segments.s174-g457: گواژه (noun) 'witness' is placed first for emphasis; بسی is an adverb/qualifier 'enough' and باشدت is an archaic optative/ desiderative-predicate 'may it be to you' (باش + enclitic -ت 'to you'), so بسی باشدت = 'may it be enough for you' or 'let it suffice you'. با + فسوس is an instrumental/locative phrase 'by/with فسوس' (فسوس is a poetic word meaning a hiss/insidious whisper/whistle) giving the manner or evidence that will suffice. Word order here is topicalized noun + predicate + instrument.
segments.s174-g458: نه is the negative particle 'not/no'; it negates the following nominal + verb clause. مرد نبردی is an archaic verbal clause: مرد (man/hero) + نبردی (past/archaic form of 'fight'/'take up arms') = 'no man fought' (negated by نه). The following و گوپال و کوس are coordinated nouns linked by و 'and' — a rhetorical list specifying who/what did not act: گوپال here is likely a proper name (or a cowherd figure) and کوس is the war-drum, so the clause reads 'neither Gupāl nor the drum.'
segments.s174-g377: به is a preposition here meaning toward/onto. برگستوان functions as a locative/nominal element indicating the place of mounting (an archaic compound); اندرون means 'within/inside' and sits before the possessed noun. اسپ گیو is a noun‑noun juxtaposition (epic word order) where اسپ = 'horse/steed' and گیو (proper name) identifies the owner — effectively 'Giv's steed.' The whole segment describes Giv coming onto (or into) the horse/saddle.
segments.s174-g378: چنان چون is a correlative simile pair meaning 'so/just as' or 'in such a way as.' بود is the past copula ('was'). ساز here means a musical tone/tune (not merely the instrument) and مردان in this epic context reads as 'men' or 'heroes' (a collective sense). نیو (ney) names the reed‑pipe whose sound is being compared. The clause states that a sound like the men's reed‑pipe was (heard).
segments.s174-g379: زره در بر places 'armor' with locative markers: در indicates 'on/in the region of' (here 'on his torso') and بر ... سرش places something 'upon his head' (بر = 'on/upon'). ش is the enclitic possessive 'his.' بود is the past copula 'was.' ترگ is a noun meaning a rag/shred placed on his head. The sequence lists his equipment/appearance in standard Persian order (thing + locative + possessor enclitic).
segments.s174-g380: دل ارغنده uses دل 'heart' followed by ارغنده as a descriptive adjective 'reddish/blood‑stained.' و links to the next phrase. تن نهاده به مرگ is a compound predication: تن 'body' + نهاده (past participle of نهاد, 'placed/laid') + به مرگ 'to death' — a terse epic way to say 'his body lay (struck) unto death' or 'his body was given over to death.' The participial phrase modifies the subject introduced earlier.
segments.s174-g381: چو functions as a temporal conjunction 'when.' از دور = 'from afar' (preposition + adverb). گرد here means 'dust' (the cloud/dust raised by the army) and سپه را is the definite object 'the army/host' marked by the object marker را. بدید is the past tense 'saw.' The clause is a temporal clause giving the occasion for the following action.
segments.s174-g382: بزد دست is an idiomatic compact construction: بزد (he struck/struck down) + دست ('hand') — 'he struck (with) his hand' or 'he smote the air/ground by hand.' و links this to the next verb. تیغ از میان برکشید contains تیغ 'sword' + از میان 'from between/out of' + برکشید, here with the verbal sense 'to draw (a weapon)' — 'he drew the sword from between (his side/sheath).' The verbs are simple past narrative forms.
segments.s174-g383: خروشی is a noun 'a roar/clamor.' برآورد here is a narrative verb 'brought forth/uttered/raised' (classical perf. form used to mean producing a sound). بر سان ابر is a simile: بر/برِ + سان 'upon/as like' + ابر 'cloud' — 'a roar like a cloud' (i.e., a thunderous roaring). The word order places the simile immediately after the action verb in epic style.
segments.s174-g384: که here introduces the consequence/result clause 'so that/that.' تاریک شد is a verb phrase 'became dark' (شد = became). مغز و چشم lists two affected faculties 'mind/brain and eye/sight.' هژبر is a proper name (the person whose faculties darkened). The whole clause expresses the effect of the roar — that Hejbar's brain and eyes were darkened (i.e., stunned/blinded).
segments.s174-g89: همی before the verb is a poetic/habitual marker meaning 'kept/continually'. گشت is the past tense of گشتن here 'to go/roam'. گرد is an adverbial/locative 'around' that follows the verb in this word order, and بیابان و کوه are the places being roamed.
segments.s174-g90: به introduces manner here (lit. 'by/with'), so به رنج و به سختی = 'with suffering and with hardship'. دور از گروه is a fixed combination meaning 'far from the group/company' (دور as adjective + از marking source). و simply coordinates the items.
segments.s174-g91: چنان بد که is a comparative/subordinate frame 'so bad that...'. روزی here functions as an adverb 'one day' (poetic placement before the predicate). پر‌اندیشه is a predicate adjective 'full of thought / troubled', and بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s174-g92: به پیشش = 'before him / in front of him' (به + پیش used locatively). یکی is the indefinite 'a certain / one', introducing بارور بیشه 'a fruitful grove'. بود is the existential/copular past 'there was'.
segments.s174-g93: بدان is a poetic locative 'to/into that' (ب + دان), followed by مرغزار 'meadow'. اندر آمد is the past verb phrase 'entered/came in', with دژم ('sullen, grim') placed after the verb as the arriving figure's description — a typical poetic inversion.
segments.s174-g94: جهان خرم is an elliptical clause 'the world (was) joyful' with the copula omitted. و links the next phrase; مرد را uses را as a topical/definite marker 'the man', دل is 'heart', and به غم (به + noun) expresses his heart's turn 'towards/into sorrow' with the copula again omitted.
segments.s174-g95: This is a sequence of direct objects seen by the subject: زمین سبز 'green earth' and چشمه پر از آب 'a spring full of water'. پر ... از is the adjectival construction 'full of', where از introduces the contained element 'water'. دید is the past verb 'saw'.
segments.s174-g96: همی again gives a continuous/emphatic sense ('indeed/continually'). جای آرامش و خواب is the noun phrase 'a place of tranquility and sleep' serving as the object of دید, with the coordinating و between آرامش and خواب. دید closes the clause as 'he saw'.
segments.s174-g417: از او شاد شد is a past-tense clause with the prepositional cause phrase (از او, “because of him”) placed before the verb phrase; شد is the past form of شدن meaning “became/was,” and شاد is the predicative adjective “glad.” The subject خسرو پاک‌دین follows the verb (poetic inversion), so the whole clause reads literally “because of him became glad Khosrow the devout.”
segments.s174-g418: ستودش is the past verb ‘praised’ with the enclitic object -ش (“him”). فراوان is an adverbial qualifier placed after the verb, modifying the praise. و کرد آفرین is a coordinate clause: کرد (did/made) plus the noun/exclamation آفرین functioning as the thing done — i.e. he uttered/expressed “afarin” (bravo).
segments.s174-g419: بخوردند is a past verb of برخوردن here meaning ‘they came upon/it befell them,’ with چیزی (‘something’) as the affected item. کجا یافتند is a short interrogative/relative phrase ‘where did they find (it)?’ — in context it is linked to بخوردند (they encountered something) and asks/notes the place of finding.
segments.s174-g420: سوی is the preposition ‘toward’; راه is the noun ‘road/path’ and بی‌راه modifies it (‘wrong, off the path’) so راه بی‌راه = ‘the wrong/off road.’ بشتافتند is the past plural of بشتافتن ‘they hastened/ran,’ so the clause means they hurried toward that mistaken/off route.
segments.s174-g421: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’; ترکان is the subject ‘the Turks.’ به نزدیک پیران is a prepositional phrase ‘to/near the elders’ (به + نزدیک + noun). شدند is the past plural of شدن ‘they became / they were,’ setting up the following adjectival predicate.
segments.s174-g422: چنان is an intensifier ‘so/thus’; a series of predicative adjectives (خسته و زار و گریان) follows and is closed by the plural past شدَند, so the adjectives jointly describe the subject (the Turks) — ‘they became so tired, wretched, and weeping.’
segments.s174-g423: برآشفت is a past verb ‘grew angry’ or ‘were upset.’ The subject پیران (the elders) follows the verb (inversion typical in poetry), and به کلباد گفت is a short clause ‘(they) said to Kalbad’ — به + proper name marks the indirect object. (Note: the provided sense list for گفت did not include the simple verb sense ‘said,’ so that sense is marked specially below.)
segments.s174-g424: که introduces the content of what was said (‘that…’). چونین = ‘such/so’; شگفتی is the noun ‘a wonder/astonishment.’ نشاید is a classical modal-negative meaning ‘it is not fitting/it must not’ (present tense/modal force), and نهفت is the participial/adjectival root meaning ‘hidden’ — together نشاید نهفت = ‘ought not be hidden’ or ‘cannot remain concealed.’
segments.s174-g409: بدو گفت is literally 'to him (بدو) said (گفت)' — a common word order in classical Persian placing the indirect object before the verb; here it means 'he said to him'. کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing the king (کای شاه = 'O king'). دل شاد دار is an imperative: شاد (happy) modifies دل (heart), and دار here functions as an imperative 'keep/hold' (classical use of دار = 'keep/let it be'), so 'keep your heart joyful.'
segments.s174-g410: خرد را is the direct object marked by را: 'your wisdom/reason'. ز اندیشه = 'from thought' (ز = 'from'), where اندیشه means thought/anxious concern. آزاد دار is another imperative clause: آزاد here is an adjective 'free', and دار again is the verbal imperative 'keep/make', so the whole phrase means 'keep (your) wisdom free from thought/worry.'
segments.s174-g411: یکی لشکر آمد is a subject + verb: 'a certain/one host came'. The phrase بر ما means 'against us' (بر + pronoun) and به جنگ gives the purpose/direction 'to battle' — so the clause reads 'a host came against us to fight.' آمد is past tense 'came.'
segments.s174-g412: چو introduces a simile/comparison 'as/like'. The two names کلباد و نستیهن are compared to the subject; تیز چنگ is an adjective + noun 'sharp/keen claws' (تیز = sharp/keen, چنگ = claw/talon), describing them or likening the attackers to those fierce figures. Word order is typical: comparison marker + examples + descriptive phrase.
segments.s174-g413: چنان بازگشتند: چنان = 'so/thus', بازگشتند is the past plural 'they returned'. The following relative clause آن کس که زیست ('that person who is alive / who survived') follows; زیست here functions as 'lives' or 'survived' in a relative sense. The sequence places the manner (چنان) and verb before the relative identifying survivors.
segments.s174-g414: که introduces the subordinate clause expressing the consequence/necessity: بر یال و برشان 'upon the mane and upon them' (بر = 'upon', یال = 'mane', برشان = 'upon them'). بباید گریست is an impersonal construction: بباید = 'it is necessary/one must' and گریست is the verbal element 'to weep/weep' in an older/formal form, so 'one must weep over their manes and over them.'
segments.s174-g415: گذشته here is a participial/perfect form 'having passed' or 'passed (by)'. ز رستم = 'from/by Rostam' (indicating point of passing), به ایران = 'into/to Iran' (direction), and سوار is a noun 'a rider/horseman' (or 'mounted man') that completes the clause: 'a rider passed by/after Rostam into Iran.'
segments.s174-g416: ندانم is the negative present 'I do not know.' که here introduces an indirect question/uncertainty ('whether' / 'that'), با من = 'with me' (i.e. against me), کند کارزار is a verbal phrase 'will make/do battle' — کند is the present/subjunctive form of کردن used to express the prospective action and کارزار = 'battle/war'. Together: 'I do not know whether he will make war with me.'
segments.s174-g161: بدو گفت is literally 'to him said' (بدو = 'to him/unto it' placed before the verb for emphasis), so it means 'he said to him.' گیو‌ای is a vocative: گیو + ای (O) addressing Giv. سر سرکشان is a title-like noun phrase 'head (سر) of the سرکشان'; the genitive relation (head of the chiefs/commanders) is expressed by placing the possessor after the head in this poetic word order.
segments.s174-g162: ز فر بزرگی چه داری نشان is a question: ز + فر بزرگی = 'of/from the great farr (royal glory),' چه داری نشان = 'what token do you have?' داری is present/past-embedded 'you have' (here functioning as 'do you possess'), and نشان is the direct object 'token/sign.' The ordering is typical Persian: prepositional phrase + question word + verb + object.
segments.s174-g163: نشان سیاوش is a possessive noun phrase 'Siavash's sign' (نشان followed by possessor سیاوش). پدیدار بود is the predicate 'became manifest/appeared' with پدیدار (apparent) plus the past copula بود ('was/appeared'). The verb is past tense, so the whole clause states a past revelation.
segments.s174-g164: چو introduces a subordinate circumstantial clause ('as/when'); بر گلستان is the locative 'on the rose-garden'; نقطه قار is a noun phrase 'a spot/point of qar' (qar here = a dark/mildew-like spot), and بود is the past copula 'was.' The clause gives the circumstance that made the sign visible.
segments.s174-g165: This is a pair of imperatives addressed to 'تو' (you): بگشای (open/reveal) and بنمای (show) — both second-person commands. بازو is the direct object of بنمای ('show your arm'), and به من marks the indirect object/recipient 'to me.' The pronoun تو is emphatic (often omitted in colloquial Persian but explicit here).
segments.s174-g166: نشان تو is a possessed noun phrase 'your token/sign.' پیداست is a fused predicative meaning 'is apparent/visible' (پیدا + است), and بر انجمن is a locative phrase 'before/at the assembly.' Word order places the possessed noun before the predicate, yielding 'your sign is visible in the assembly.'
segments.s174-g167: برهنه تن خویش is the noun phrase 'his naked body' (برهنه = naked, تن = body, خویش = his own), followed by the verb بنمود 'showed/revealed.' شاه follows the verb for emphasis ('the king showed his naked body'). The verb is past tense, so this is a past narrative action.
segments.s174-g168: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction meaning 'fixed his gaze/looked' (نگه = gaze/watch + کرد = did/made), and گیو is the actor 'Giv.' آن نشان سیاه is the direct object 'that black sign/mark.' The verb+object order and placement of the subject are poetically inverted but follow Persian permissive word-order for emphasis.
segments.s174-g153: سرانجام (adverb) = 'at last / finally' starts the clause; کیخسرو is the subject; آید پدید is a poetic, slightly inverted verb phrase where آید (present/epic present 'comes / appears') combines with پدید ('to appear, to come into view') — word order is emphatic/poetic (verb after subject, and the two verbs together give the sense 'comes forth / appears').
segments.s174-g154: بجا آورد is a light/verbal phrase meaning 'brought (at the right time) / produced'; the following phrase بندها را کلید is a poetic inversion: بندها را marks the definite/focused 'bonds/fetters' and کلید (placed after را) is the direct nominal object 'the key (to those bonds)'. In modern order it would be کلید بندها را آورد — here the order stresses timing (بجا) and the key.
segments.s174-g155: بدانگه 'thereupon / at that time' sets the temporal frame; که introduces the subordinate clause 'when/that'; گردد is the present/subjunctive/passive-ish form meaning 'becomes / is made'; جهاندار is a title 'world‑ruler / sovereign'; نیو here is an archaic/poetic spelling meaning 'new' (نو), so the whole clause means 'when the sovereign is made new / reestablished.'
segments.s174-g156: ز ایران 'from Iran' (z = from, poetic); بیاید here is the present/epic-form of آمدن 'comes / will come' (used to state a forthcoming arrival); سرافراز is an adjective 'lofty / triumphant / honored' modifying گیو (proper name) which follows — standard Persian order places the adjective before the name in poetry for emphasis.
segments.s174-g157: مر is an older/dialectal dative 'to me' placed up front for emphasis; او را is the definite direct object 'him'; سوی تخت ایران is a directional phrase 'toward the throne of Iran'; برد is the past of بردن 'took / brought' — the whole clause means 'He brought him to me toward the Iranian throne.'
segments.s174-g158: بر here functions as 'before / in the presence of / to' (directional/punctual sense); نامداران و شیران is a compound 'the famed/renowned (men) and the lions' (شیران = heroic men); برد again is 'he led / carried (him)'. The clause thus says he presented/led him before the nobles and the warriors.
segments.s174-g159: جهان را is the definite object 'the world'; به مردی 'to/for a man' indicates beneficiary or locus; به پای آورد is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'to bring to (someone's) feet / to place under someone's feet' — together 'he placed the world at a man's feet' (i.e., subdued the world for him).
segments.s174-g160: همان 'that very / the same' points back emphatically; کین ما را 'our vengeance' is the definite object (را); به جای آورد is an idiom meaning 'to put in place / to accomplish / to fulfill' — here it means 'to execute / make good (our vengeance)', i.e. 'he exacted that vengeance of ours.'
segments.s174-g241: This is a nominal clause: the head noun 'گله' (flock/herd) is followed by the quantifying relative 'هرچه' (‘whatever’/‘all that’), then the copula/existential 'هست' ('are' / 'exist') and a locative prepositional phrase 'اندر آن مرغزار' ('in that meadow'). Word order is Subject + relative + copula + locative, typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s174-g242: This is a verbal clause indicating motion: the preposition 'به' introduces the goal ('to/towards'), followed by 'آبشخور' (watering-place) as the immediate destination; 'آید' is the present/subjunctive/indicative 3rd-person singular of 'آمدن' (here 'comes'); 'سوی' reinforces direction ('toward(s)') and 'جویبار' names the stream. Order: to‑goal + verb + directional phrase.
segments.s174-g243: 'به بهزاد' is the indirect object ('to Behzad'); 'بنمای' is an imperative form meaning 'show!' (classical/binominal imperative built on نمای-). The direct objects 'زین و لگام' ('saddle and bridle') follow; the coordinating 'و' links them. Order: to + addressee, verb-imperative, then objects.
segments.s174-g244: 'چو' = 'when'; 'او' = 'he/it'; 'رام گردد' is a two-word predicate meaning 'becomes tame' ('رام' adjective + 'گردد' = become); 'تو' is the addressed second person 'you'; 'بگذار گام' is an idiomatic instruction literally 'place/put (your) step' — here 'بگذار' (imperative/second-person) + 'گام' (step) = 'take your step / proceed'.
segments.s174-g245: 'چو آیی' = 'when you come' (چو + present/subjunctive/2nd-sing 'آیی'). 'برش' is an enclitic/archaic contraction meaning 'to him' (bound pronoun attached to a prepositional element); here it indicates the person approached. 'نیک' qualifies the following verb phrase ('well/pleasantly'); 'بنمای' is the imperative 'show/reveal' and 'چهر' is the direct object 'face/appearance' — so: 'when you come to him, show (him) a good/pleasant face.'
segments.s174-g246: Two coordinated verbs express actions toward that face: 'بیارای' (classical verb form meaning 'adorn/beautify' — here an instruction or hortative) and 'ببسای' (an optative/subjunctive form meaning roughly 'may it avail/please' or 'make it pleasing'); 'رویِ ش' = 'his face' (روی + enclitic 'ش' = his), and 'به مهر' indicates the instrument/attitude 'with love/affection' (به + مهر). Together: 'adorn it and make his face pleasing with affection.'
segments.s174-g247: 'سیاوش' is the subject/name; 'چو' = 'when'; 'گشت' is the past-tense/simple perfect of 'گشتن' meaning 'became'; 'از جهان' is a prepositional phrase 'of/from the world' and 'ناامید' is the predicate adjective 'hopeless/despairing'. Structure: Name + temporal 'when' + verb + complement indicating source and resulting state.
segments.s174-g248: 'بر او' = 'toward him'/'for him' (the effect falls on him); 'تیره شد' = 'became dark' ('تیره' adjective + شد = became); 'روی روز سپید' is a poetic compound meaning 'the bright face of the day' (روی = face, روز = day, سپید qualifying 'روز' as 'bright/white/pure'). Word order: dative phrase (for him) + predicate + subject noun phrase describing the day’s bright face that grows dark.
segments.s174-g225: This noun phrase is the direct object: 'یکی را' (object-marked 'one / someone') + possessive specification 'ز ما' ('of us'), followed by postnominal modifiers 'زنده اندر جهان' ('alive in the world'). In classical/poetic Persian the object (with را) can precede the verb, and the preposition ز appears before the pronoun to indicate 'of/from us.'
segments.s174-g226: نبیند is a third-person present/subjunctive form used here in an indefinite/negative sense; together with کسی before it it yields the meaning 'no one sees.' 'آشکار و نهان' are adverbial/adjunct pairs 'openly and secretly' describing the manner of seeing. Word order places the verb clause after the object phrase from the previous segment.
segments.s174-g227: This is a copular clause: 'جهان' (subject) + predicate 'پر ز ... است' meaning 'is full of ...'. The construction 'پر ز X' is poetic for 'full of X.' The two nouns 'بدخواه و پردشمن' are coordinated objects of the 'full of' phrase.
segments.s174-g228: 'همه مرز ما' is a noun phrase meaning 'all our borders' (note Persian often places the possessor pronoun after the noun: 'مرز ما' = 'our border'). 'جای آهرمن است' is the predicate 'is the place of Ahriman' — an equative statement identifying those borders as Ahriman's domain.
segments.s174-g229: 'تو‌ای' is a vocative 'O you'; the epithets follow: 'بافرین شاه' ('praiseworthy/glorious king') and 'فرزند من' ('my son'), where the possessive pronoun 'من' follows the noun to mean 'my.' This is an address combining title and kinship.
segments.s174-g230: نگر is an imperative 'look!' followed by a purpose clause introduced by تا meaning 'so that / in order that.' نیوشی is the second‑person present/subjunctive of نیوشیدن 'to hear/receive (word)', giving 'that you may hear.' 'یکی پند من' is the object 'one piece of my advice' (یکی = numeral 'one' modifying 'پند', with 'من' as the possessive 'my' after the noun).
segments.s174-g231: که‌گر is a conditional 'if.' آگهی یابد is a verbal phrase 'obtains information / learns (of it)' — یابد is third‑person present/subjunctive used for the protasis. 'آن مرد شوم' is the noun phrase 'that ill‑omened / wicked man,' where شوم is an adjectival epithet.
segments.s174-g232: برانگیزد is a third‑person verb 'he will arouse / set in motion' (here with the sense 'kindle / set ablaze'). آتش is the direct object 'fire.' 'ز آباد بوم' is a prepositional phrase 'from/in the prosperous/inhabited homeland' specifying where the fire is kindled; آباد modifies بوم ('prosperous homeland').
segments.s174-g193: همی is a classical progressive/adverbial particle meaning ‘kept on / was continually’; گفت is the past tense ‘said’ (subject omitted, ‘he’). با شاه marks the addressee ‘to/with the king’. یکسر سخن is an adverbial phrase (‘one-straight speech’ → ‘plainly/straightforwardly’), placed after the verb cluster and modifying how he spoke.
segments.s174-g194: که introduces a reported/subordinate clause (‘that’). دادار گیتی is a genitive/construct phrase ‘the Creator of the world’ (دادار = creator, گیتی = world). چه is the interrogative ‘what’ inside the subordinate clause; افگند is the past form of افکندن (‘cast/laid’). بن is a noun ‘foundation/root’ and completes the object of افگند; word order is typical older Persian (verb before object in poetic inversion).
segments.s174-g195: همان is a demonstrative determiner ‘that/the same’ pointing back to the previously mentioned dream. خواب گودرز is a possessive/construct ‘Goudarz’s dream’. و links رنج دراز: رنج is the noun ‘suffering’ and دراز is a postpositive adjective ‘long/extended’, so ‘long suffering’.
segments.s174-g196: A coordinate list introduced simply by juxtaposition and و: خور (here nominal, ‘food/eating’), پوشش ‘covering/cloak’ (i.e. clothing), درد ‘pain/sorrow’, آرام used as a noun ‘rest/calm’, and ناز ‘pampering/indulgence’. The repeated و simply links these items as a catalogue of miseries and comforts.
segments.s174-g197: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/because of’. کاووس is the proper name ‘Kavus’. کش here functions in a compound (کش‌سال) implying duration/extension (‘long-lasting’); سال is ‘year’. بفگند is a past-tense verb ‘cast/put down’ (poetic form of افکند), and فر is the noun ‘farr’ (royal divine glory). The clause says that, owing to Kavus’s prolonged years, the farr was cast down.
segments.s174-g198: ز again ‘from/because of’. درد is the cause ‘from pain’. پسر is the affected noun ‘the son’ (here ‘the young man/prince’). گشت is the past of شدن ‘became’. بی is the preposition ‘without’ and governs پای (foot) and پر (feather/wing) joined by و; the idiom means he was left incapable/disabled — literally ‘became without foot and wing’. Word order places the cause first, then the affected person and result.
segments.s174-g199: ز ایران ‘from Iran’ indicates source or domain. پراکنده شد is a verbal phrase ‘was scattered/dispersed’ (پراکنده adjective + شد auxiliary). رنگ و بوی (‘color and scent’) is a conventional pair meaning the nation's vitality/appearance; they are the grammatical subject of پراکنده شد.
segments.s174-g200: سراسر is an adverb ‘throughout/entirely’. به ویرانی آورد is a causative/result phrase ‘brought to ruin’ (آورد = ‘bring/cause’), and روی here means ‘face/appearance/countenance’. The whole unit says his countenance/appearance was everywhere/entirely brought to ruin.
segments.s174-g353: نماند is the past tense verb ماند with the negative prefix ن- = 'did not remain'. Poetic word order places the verb before its complements. یک زمان ('a/one moment') gives the short duration and در نهفت ('hidden in') is a verbless locative phrase: lit. 'in hiddenness'; together the phrase means 'did not remain hidden for even a moment.'
segments.s174-g354: This is a simple narrative clause: کس آمد 'someone came' (کس = an indefinite person) + به نزدیک پیران 'to the vicinity/presence of the elders' (prepositional phrase) + بگفت, a past-reporting verb used in classical Persian meaning 'he said' or 'told'. Word order is subject + verb + directional phrase + reporting verb.
segments.s174-g355: که introduces a relative/clarifying clause here ('that/who'); آمد is the past tense 'came'. ز ایران is the poetic preposition 'from Iran'. سرافراز is an epithet meaning 'honored/triumphant' modifying گیو (Giv). The clause therefore identifies who came: 'Giv, triumphant from Iran.'
segments.s174-g356: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the presence of' and functions as direction. بیدار دل is a compound epithet 'wakeful‑hearted' (بیدار modifying دل) describing the شاه (king). نیو here appears as a proper name/title following شاه. The whole phrase reads 'to the presence of the wakeful‑hearted king Niu.'
segments.s174-g357: سوی شهر ایران is the directional phrase 'toward the city of Iran'. نهادند is the past plural form of نهادن 'to place/set' used idiomatically with روی ('face') in نهادند روی = 'they set/turned their faces'. The verb follows the direction in this poetic order.
segments.s174-g358: A coordinated list introduced by and (و): فرنگیس و شاه و گو جنگ‌جوی names the persons who turned toward Iran. گو here is most likely a proper name (not the verb 'say'), and جنگ‌جوی is an appositive 'the warrior'. The order is nominal coordination without an explicit verb because the verb was in the previous segment.
segments.s174-g359: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بشنید is past 'heard' with پیران (the elders) as subject. غمی گشت is a result clause 'became sorrowful/grieved', where گشت is the past of گشتن 'became' and سخت functions as an intensifier/adverb 'very/strongly'. The whole means 'when the elders heard, they became greatly sorrowful.'
segments.s174-g360: بلرزید is the past tense 'trembled'. بر سان is a comparative construction 'upon/like' (بر + سان = 'like upon'), followed by برگ درخت 'a leaf of the tree'. The phrase is a simile: 'they trembled like a leaf on a tree.'
segments.s174-g57: چو here is a subordinating conjunction meaning “when.” گفتی is the 2nd-person past (you said) introducing reported speech. ندارم is the first-person present-negative “I do not have” (here quoted speech: “I have no …”). ز + شاه = “of/from the king,” with آگهی (“news, information”) as the thing not had. Word order mirrors Persian: temporal clause (چو گفتی) + quoted clause (ندارم ز شاه آگهی).
segments.s174-g58: تنش را is “his body” with the object marker را (تن + enclitic ش = his body + را). ز جان is a genitive phrase “of life” (lit. ‘from life’). زود کردی تهی is a past action: کردی (you made/did) + تهی (empty/devoid), with زود as an adverb “quickly/soon.” The overall structure is verb-final typical Persian: (object) … (adverb) + کردی + predicate adjective تهی.
segments.s174-g59: به خم کمندش is a locative phrase: به “on/to” + خم “bend/curve” + کمندش “his snare/loop” (کمند + enclitic ش). بیاویختی is a 2nd-person past/preterite (archaic/poetic form) meaning “you hung (up).” The clause places the locative phrase before the verb, as is natural in Persian poetry.
segments.s174-g60: سبک here functions adverbially “lightly” or “with little weight.” از برش should be read as از + بر + -ش (from/over him; here بر + enclitic ش = “on him”), followed by خاک “dust” and بر‌بیختی, an archaic past form meaning “(you) cast/threw upon” or “was cast upon.” Together: “lightly, you cast dust upon him.” Note the poetic/arhaic verb form and the enclitic -ش attaching to the preposition.
segments.s174-g61: بدان is a fused form (ب- + دان) functioning like “by that / with that” or “so” introducing purpose/result. تا introduces the purpose clause “so that / in order that.” نداند is the negative present/subjunctive meaning “may not know” or “should not know.” کسی is indefinite (“anyone”), and راز او is the object “his secret.” The clause expresses purpose: (he acted) so that no one would learn his secret.
segments.s174-g62: همان points back to that secret (“that/that very [secret]”). نشنود is the negative present/subjunctive of شنیدن “to hear” (meaning “may not hear” / “should not hear”). نام و آواز او is a compound object “his name and voice,” i.e. identity or reputation. The verb is subordinate and expresses desired non-disclosure.
segments.s174-g63: یکی را is an indefinite object “someone” + object marker را. همی برد is a habitual/iterative present/past-interpretation construction in poetic Persian: همی (continuous particle) + برد (root ‘to take’), here meaning “he would take” / “he took.” با خویشتن means “with himself” (reflexive accompaniment). Note that برد in the provided sense list is not the verb sense, so treat it as the verb ‘to take’ in context.
segments.s174-g64: ورا is the allative/indirect-object form “to him/for him.” رهنمون is a predicate noun “guide,” and بود is the past copula “was.” زآن انجمن is a prepositional phrase “from/out of that assembly” (زآن = from that, انجمن = assembly/gathering). The clause says the person taken served as his guide out of that group.
segments.s174-g313: دو رخ را is a direct-object phrase: رخ (here 'cheeks') with the object marker را. The verb phrase بر نهاد (past of نهادن) means 'placed/laid upon'; it takes the prepositional phrase به یال و برش ('upon/against the mane and its crest/section') as its complement. Word order is object (دو رخ را) + prepositional target (به یال و برش) + past verb (بر نهاد), a common poetic inversion.
segments.s174-g314: ز indicates cause ('because of / from'); درد سیاوش is a genitive phrase 'Siavash's pain/sorrow'. بسی is an adverb here ('much/greatly' in poetic usage). کند + یاد would be a compound verb 'to remember'; here کرد (past of کردن) with یاد yields 'remembered.' The clause thus means 'he remembered much on account of Siavash's suffering.'
segments.s174-g315: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when/as'. آب دو دیده is a genitive phrase literally 'the water of the two eyes' (a poetic expression for tears). پراگنده کرد uses کرد as a light verb with the adjective 'پراگنده' ('scattered, dispersed') to mean 'caused to scatter' or simply 'shed/spread' (here: tears falling).
segments.s174-g316: سبک‌سر is an adverb/adjective 'rashly' or 'light‑mindedly' modifying the action. سوی + کردن is a verbal construction meaning 'to turn toward' or 'to go toward'; گنج آگنده is the goal phrase 'the treasure that is full/filled.' کرد is the past tense 'did/turned'. Together: '(she) rashly turned toward the full treasure.'
segments.s174-g317: به ایوان marks location ('in/at the iwan'); یکی گنج is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain treasure' or 'one treasure'. بودش is the past of بودن with enclitic -ش ('it was/there was'), and نهان is the predicate adjective 'hidden.' The clause states existence/location: 'In the iwan a certain treasure was hidden.'
segments.s174-g318: نبد is a negative existential/preterite 'there was not' (archaic/poetic form). زآن ('of that' / 'about it') links to the prior clause. کسی آگه اندر جهان functions together as 'anyone aware in the world' — with the negation it yields 'there was no one in the world aware of it.' Note the classical word order (negation + source phrase + person + predicate).
segments.s174-g319: یکی گنج آگنده is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain treasure, full/filled' (آگنده = filled). دینار functions as a genitive/specifier indicating the treasure's contents ('of dinars'). بود is the simple past 'was' stating existence: 'There was a treasure filled with dinars.'
segments.s174-g320: This is a simple list of items with repeated past-tense copulas: زره بود 'there was armor' ; و یاقوت بسیار بود 'and there were many rubies.' بسیاری/بسیار here works as a quantifier 'many' modifying یاقوت. Repetition of بود is typical in classical Persian narrative enumeration.
segments.s174-g41: که opens reported speech here ('that'); با او بگفت literally 'said with him' = 'said to him' (با + third-person pronoun used for the indirect object). بگفت is the past tense (archaic/poetic b‑prefix) meaning 'he said'. آن‌که begins a relative/nominal clause 'the one who / that which'; جز تو کس است = 'there is no one except you' (جز = 'except', کس = 'anyone/person').
segments.s174-g42: که here continues the relative clause 'who/that'; اندر جهان = 'in the world' (اندر = poetic 'in/within'). کردگار is 'the Creator/God' (title), او is the pronoun referring to that Creator, and بس است is a predicative phrase 'is enough / is sufficient' (بس = 'enough', است = 'is'), so the clause means 'who, in the world, the Creator deems sufficient.'
segments.s174-g43: This is a nominal phrase listing celestial bodies with خداوند as their lord/title: 'خداوند خورشید و کیوان و ماه' = 'the Lord of the sun, Kiwan (Saturn) and the moon.' Word order is noun + modifiers (typical in Persian poetry).
segments.s174-g44: کز = 'from which/from whom' (here 'from him'); اوی is a classical/pronominal form referring back to that lord ('him'); است is copula 'is/are'; پیروزی و دستگاه are the governed nouns 'victory and dominion/establishment' — together: 'from him are victory and dominion.'
segments.s174-g45: خداوند here as 'God/Lord'; هستی = 'existence/being' and راستی = 'truth/uprightness' connected by و هم ('and also' / emphatic 'and'); the phrase names God's domains: 'Lord of existence and (also) of truth.'
segments.s174-g46: نخواهد is negative future/modal 'will not' (classical usage of خواهد); ز تو = 'from you' (ز = poetic 'from'); کژی و کاستی = 'crookedness and deficiency/shortcoming' — together: 'He will not (accept/allow) crookedness or deficiency from you.'
segments.s174-g47: جز از رای و فرمان او = 'except by/from his counsel and command' (جز = 'except', از = 'from/by', رای = 'counsel/plan', فرمان = 'command', او = 'his'); راه نیست is the idiom 'there is no way/other course' (راه = 'way', نیست = 'is not/there is none'), so 'there is no way except by his counsel and command.'
segments.s174-g48: خور و ماه = 'the sun and the moon' (خور = poetic/short form of خورشید); از این دانش آگاه نیست = 'are not aware of this knowledge' (از = 'of/regarding', این دانش = 'this knowledge', آگاه نیست = 'is not aware'), so literally 'the sun and moon are not aware of this knowledge.'
segments.s174-g449: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now' placed at the front. گیو را uses را as the definite/object marker marking 'Giv' as the direct object. ساختی is 2nd-person past ('you made/formed'); it governs the following object phrase پیل مست ('an elephant, intoxicated'), where پیل is the noun and مست is a postpositive adjective (poetic; literally 'intoxicated' or 'wild').
segments.s174-g450: میان یلان is a prepositional phrase 'among the yelān (heroes)'; گشت is the past/preterite of گشتن here meaning 'became/turned (to be)'. نام تو is the possessor construction 'your name/reputation' (noun + pronoun); پست is the predicative adjective 'low/dishonoured' completing the verbless/result state introduced by گشت.
segments.s174-g451: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. ز این ('of/from this') links the following verb to the foregoing fact. یابد is 3rd-person present/subjunctive of یافتن used here as 'learns/comes to know'. افراسیاب is the subject proper name and آگهی is the object meaning 'news/information' — together: 'when Afrasiab learns this news.'
segments.s174-g452: بیندازد is a 3rd-person verb with the prefix بیـ (here functioning as the present/subjunctive/future-like form) meaning 'he casts/throws down'. آن تاج شاهنشهی is a noun phrase 'that crown of kingship/imperial crown' with آن as demonstrative + تاج 'crown' + شاهنشهی qualifying it as royal/imperial.
segments.s174-g453: که here functions as a relative/conjunctive particle linking to what follows ('for/that'), introducing the description: دو پهلوان 'two champions' with دلیر 'brave' as an adjective and و سوار adding 'and mounted/horsemen' — an appositive descriptive string describing the champions.
segments.s174-g454: چنین is an adjective/adverb 'such' modifying لشکری 'an army/troops'. از در کارزار is a fixed locative/comitative phrase 'in/on the field of battle' (از + در forming a poetic locative), so the whole segment situates that army on the battlefield.
segments.s174-g455: ز پیش = 'formerly/earlier' (literally 'from before'). سواری here is 'horsemen/cavalry'. نمودید is 2nd-person plural/formal past 'you showed/did show'; پشت is the noun 'back' used as the object of نمودن → 'showed (your) back' (i.e. turned your back to the horsemen).
segments.s174-g456: بسی is an adverbial intensifier here ('many/much' in older usage), followed by از دلیران ترکان 'of the brave Turks' (از marking the group). بکشت is an archaic perfective form (b- prefix + root) used here to mean 'he/you slew/killed' in epic narrative; the phrase therefore says '(you) killed many of the brave Turks.'
segments.s174-g305: ز بالا (prepositional phrase) = 'from above/upstairs'; followed by به ایوان 'toward the iwan/veranda'. نهادند is past 3rd‑person plural of نهادن 'to place/set' and here combines with روی (literally 'they set [their] face(s)') — a poetic idiom meaning 'they turned/set their faces toward'. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic rhythm: 'from above to the iwan they placed (their) face'.
segments.s174-g306: پر‌اندیشه is an adjectival compound 'full of thought/reflective' modifying the implied subject; مغز و روان is a paired noun phrase 'brain (mind) and soul', linked by و. راه‌جوی is an agent noun 'seeker/traveler' in apposition, describing their mental and spiritual state — literally 'thoughtful, mind and soul, seekers of the road'.
segments.s174-g307: چو ... رفتند = 'when they went' (چو as a temporal conjunction). نزد فرنگیس = 'to/in the presence of Ferangis' (نزد = near/in the company of). باز after the verb means 'again' or 'once more' — the word order places باز after رفتند for emphasis: 'they went again/they went and again...'.
segments.s174-g308: سخن رفت is an impersonal or intransitive poetic idiom meaning 'talk/conversation moved/turned' (i.e., discussion began or resumed). چندی is an adverb 'for a while'; ز here means 'about/concerning' and governs راه دراز 'the long road' (دراز modifying راه). The phrase thus reads 'conversation (turned) for a while to the long road.'
segments.s174-g309: بدان تا is a classical collocation meaning 'so that' or 'in order that' (ب + دان + تا functioning together as a subordinating phrase). نهانی بود کارشان = 'their business/affair was hidden' (نهانی = hidden/secret, بود = past copula 'was', کارشان = 'their affair'). The phrase expresses purpose: 'so that their affair remained secret.'
segments.s174-g310: نباشد is a negative subjunctive/optative form meaning 'there should not be' or 'may there not be' (here conveying 'so that there be no...'). کسی in the scope of this negation functions as 'no one'. آگه از رازشان = 'aware of their secret' (آگه = aware, از = 'of/concerning', رازشان = their secret). Together: 'that no one be aware of their secret.'
segments.s174-g311: چون ... دید = 'when she saw' (چون used here as a temporal 'when'); نزد is absent here, so 'روی بهزاد دید' = 'saw Behzad's face'. Word order is straightforward subject (فرنگیس) + temporal conjunction + object phrase + verb.
segments.s174-g312: شد ... ناپدید is a verbal phrase 'became/was made ... invisible/vanished' (شد = became, ناپدید = vanished). از آب‌دیده is an ablative/causal phrase 'because of (her) tear‑wet eyes' (از = 'because of/from', آب‌دیده = 'water‑seen/tearful' i.e., eyes filled with tears). رخش here means 'her countenance/beauty' (the -ش suffix is the possessive 'her/his' resolved in context to Ferangis). The whole line: 'her countenance became vanished because of tearful eyes.'
segments.s174-g425: Two short interrogative clauses run together. کردید is the 2nd-person plural past of کردن (“you did”), with an implied subject 'you'; با marks accompaniment ("with Giv"). The coordinate و joins خسرو, and کجاست is the interrogative copula formed from کجا ("where") + ست (archaic/poetic form of است, "is").
segments.s174-g426: سخن is the topic 'the speech/report'. بر introduces the phrase 'about/regarding' (here roughly 'as to'), and چه سان است is a fixed interrogative 'in what manner/how is it'. برگوی is an imperative/first-personal-root form 'speak/tell' (here a request/command), and راست is the adverb/adjective 'true/straight' modifying the speech act ('speak truly').
segments.s174-g427: بدو is the dative 'to him/unto him' (word order V + indirect object). گفت here functions as the speech verb ('said') with کلباد as the speaker following the verb (verb–subject inversion is common in epic style). کای پهلوان is a vocative address: 'O champion'.
segments.s174-g428: به پیش = 'in the presence of / before'; توگر is the conditional particle ('if') attached to the second person form in older Persian usage; برگشایم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive 'I open/reveal' (بر + گشایم), and زبان is the object 'tongue/speech' meaning 'my speech' or 'my words'. The clause is conditional: 'If I open my tongue before you...'.
segments.s174-g429: که introduces a subordinate content/relative clause ('that/what'), followed by the subject + epithet 'گیو دلاور' (Giv the brave). به گردان is a prepositional phrase 'to/with the troop', and چه کرد is the interrogative 'what did he do' (چه = what; کرد = did). The clause asks/introduces 'what Giv did regarding the troop'.
segments.s174-g430: دلت is دل + enclitic -ت 'your heart'. سیر گردَد uses سیر ('sated/fulness') with گردد (subjunctive/optative 'may become' / 'become') to express a wish/result ('may your heart become satisfied'). به دشت نبرد is a locative phrase 'in/with the plain (field) of battle' giving the cause/scene of the satisfaction.
segments.s174-g431: فراوان is an adverb 'often/abundantly' modifying دیده‌ای ('you have seen'). به لشکر here means 'in/among the host' or 'my host' depending on scansion; لشکر + possessive suffix (written here as separate م) gives 'my army' and را is the object marker. دیده‌ای is 2nd-person perfect 'you have seen'.
segments.s174-g432: نبرد مرا combines نبرد ('battle') with the possessive suffix -م ('my', here represented as م) plus the object marker را, forming 'my battle' as the object. هم 'also' or 'likewise' modifies پسندیده‌ای, which is 2nd-person perfect 'you have approved/liked'. The overall meaning: 'You have also approved my battle.'
segments.s174-g345: چو here is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause; این refers to the foregoing matter ('this'). کرده شد is a perfect/passive-style verbal phrase: کرده (done) + شد (auxiliary 'be') giving 'had been done' or 'was done'. برنهادند is a past-tense, 3rd-person-plural verb 'they lifted/raised/put on', with the following noun زین as its object.
segments.s174-g346: بر introduces the target onto which something is placed ('upon/onto'); آن points to those previously mentioned items; بادپایان (بادپا + -ان) is an epithet 'the fleet-footed / swift runners' (here likely horses or riders). با‌آفرین is an adverbial phrase 'with praise' (i.e. accompanied by praise or applause). The whole phrase modifies/completes برنهادند زین ('they put the saddle on those swift-footed ones, with praise').
segments.s174-g347: Subject-first word order: فرنگیس (name) is the subject. ترگی 'a rag/kerchief' is the direct object, and به سر ('on the head') is the locative phrase specifying where it was placed. بر نهاد is the past tense of نهادن with the preverb بر forming the compound verb 'put on/placed on' (3rd-person-singular here).
segments.s174-g348: برفتند is a 3rd-person-plural past 'they departed/rode off.' هر سه ('each/ all three') specifies the participants. به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner of' and باد is a simile 'wind' — together 'in the manner of the wind' = 'like the wind.' The whole phrase gives the manner of their departure.
segments.s174-g349: سران is the subject 'the chiefs/heads/leading ones.' سوی ایران is a directional phrase 'toward Iran.' نهادند is the past of نهادن 'they set/they placed' used here intransitively to mean 'they directed themselves/they set out.' گرم is a predicative/adverbial adjective 'warm, eager' indicating the manner or zeal of the action.
segments.s174-g350: نهانی is an adverb 'secretly.' چنان چون بود is a small clause 'so/as it was' (چنان and چون forming an emphatic 'such as' + بود the past copula). نرم نرم is a reduplicated adverb 'softly / gently, gently' for emphasis; together the phrase describes the manner (quietly and gently) of whatever is being referred to.
segments.s174-g351: بشد here is the past/auxiliary meaning 'it became' (resultative). شهر 'the city' is the subject, یکسر 'entirely' is an adverb modifying the degree, and پر از 'full of' takes the noun phrase گفت و گوی ('talk and conversation') as its complement; گفت و گو is the conventional compound meaning 'conversation.'
segments.s174-g352: که introduces a content/relative clause 'that ...'. خسرو is the subject (Khosrow). به ایران is the directional complement 'toward Iran.' نهاده‌ست is the perfective periphrasis 'has placed / has set' (archaic literary present perfect), and روی 'face' completes the idiom نهادنِ روی — 'to set one's face toward' = 'to make for / to head for' Iran.
segments.s174-g281: کنون is a fronted temporal adverb 'now'. جان خسرو is a genitive/possessive noun phrase ('the life of Khosrow'), with خسرو postpositively specifying جان. شد is a past/copulative verb here conveying change/state ('was/has gone' or 'became [so]'); the clause then links with و + رنج من ('and my suffering') as a coordinated second member.
segments.s174-g282: همین رنج بد is a noun phrase 'this very bad/evil suffering' (همین = 'this/same' emphasizing immediacy). در جهان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the world' modifying گنج من; the copula ('is') is omitted (typical poetic elision), so گنج من ('my treasure') is the predicate nominal linked to the preceding phrase.
segments.s174-g283: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'). یک نیمه ببرید زآن کوه is a past action clause meaning 'one half was cut from that mountain' (ببرید is a past-form verb here; word order is flexible/poetic). شاه appears at the end of the segment but functions as the subject/topic that links to the main clause in the next segment.
segments.s174-g284: گران کرد is a verbal complex 'made heavy' (گران used as adjective/secondary predicate and کرد the past verb 'made/did'). باز is the adverb 'again' modifying کرد. آن عنان سیاه is the object phrase 'that black rein' (عنان 'reins/bridle', سیاه postpositive adjective 'black'); overall: 'he again made that black rein heavy.'
segments.s174-g285: همی بود is an iterative/past expression 'it was continuing' or 'it kept on.' تا introduces the temporal limit 'until' and پیش او رفت is the subordinate clause 'went before him/in his presence.' The proper name گیو follows the verb in typical poetic inversion and is the actor/subject of رفت.
segments.s174-g286: چنین گفت is a formulaic phrase 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said'); what follows is the address. بیدار دل is a compound adjective 'wakeful‑hearted' qualifying شاه ('king') — Persian often places such adjective+noun units in this packed order in poetry — and نیو appears as a name or vocative appended to the address. The clause functions as the speech-introduction.
segments.s174-g287: The initial که introduces a subordinate content clause ('that' / 'so that'), followed by شاید 'perhaps' marking possibility. The second که is an emphatic/continuative conjunction. اندیشه پهلوان is a nominal phrase 'the thought/intention of a champion' (اندیشه as 'intention/plan' with پهلوان specifying the object of that thought); the verb completing this idea follows in the next segment.
segments.s174-g288: کنم is first‑person singular (subjunctive/optative) 'I may do / I will do' and is governed by شاید earlier ('perhaps I may...'). آشکارا is an adverb 'openly/evidently' modifying کنَم. به روشن‌روان is a prepositional phrase 'for/to the bright‑souled one' (روشن‌روان an epithet meaning 'bright‑soul' or 'clear‑souled,' here the beneficiary or addressee of the action).
segments.s174-g401: به is the preposition 'to'. نستیهن is a proper name (Nastīhan). گرد here is a poetic use meaning 'circle/retinue' or 'around' (it links the name to those gathered around him). The verb clause کلباد گفت places the speaker (Kalbad) after the object phrase — گفت is the simple past (3rd person) 'said'.
segments.s174-g402: که introduces reported speech 'that'. این کوه is 'this mountain'. خارا‌ست is the fused adjective + copula meaning 'is rocky / of hard stone' (خارا + است). نه introduces a contrast 'not'; یال و سفت lists two nouns/adjectives ('mane' and 'firm/hard') to mark what the mountain is not.
segments.s174-g403: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject. خسته 'tired/exhausted' and بسته 'bound/tied' are predicative adjectives/participles linked by و. گشتند is the past plural of گشتن used as 'became/turned' or 'they went/returned' here, and باز at the end means 'again' / 'back', marking the return.
segments.s174-g404: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to (the) near/nearby'. پیران is the plural noun 'elders'. گردن‌فراز is an epithet 'proud' (literally 'neck-high' / 'high-necked') modifying پیران; together they mean 'the proud elders' and indicate toward whom they returned.
segments.s174-g405: همه here quantifies 'غار و هامون' meaning 'every/all the caves and plains'. پر از کشته بود is a predicate construction: پر از 'full of' + کشته 'the slain (bodies)' + بود 'was' (past copula), so 'were full of slain'.
segments.s174-g406: ز خون introduces cause/source 'from (the) blood'. خاک is 'the earth/soil'. چون here functions 'like/as' and ارغوان 'crimson/purple' is the simile. گشته بود is the compound past ('had become' / 'was turned'), so the clause means 'the earth, from blood, had become like crimson.'
segments.s174-g407: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نزدیک آمد 'drew near/came near' — here نزدیک is adverbial 'near' and آمد is the past 'came'. کیخسرو is the subject (Kay Khosrow); دلیر 'brave' is an adjective placed after the verb for poetic emphasis, qualifying the subject.
segments.s174-g408: پر از خون is a participial/descriptive phrase 'full of blood' describing him. بر ... و ... بر uses the preposition بر 'with/on/upon' to list what he bears — here بر + چنگ (claw) gives 'with (sword) and with claw'; سان شیر is the simile 'like a lion'. Altogether: 'covered in blood, with sword and claw like a lion.'
segments.s174-g385: میان سواران is a prepositional phrase meaning "among the riders" with the noun سواران following میان. بیامد is the past-tense third-person singular (archaic/poetic prefixed form of آمد) meaning "he came." چو introduces a simile and links to گرد, where گرد here denotes a dust-whirl or whirlwind; the simile modifies the verb بیامد ("he came like a dust-storm").
segments.s174-g386: ز پرخاش او is a causal/ablative phrase: ز here means "from/because of" and links the cause to the following clause. پرخاش او = "his onslaught/assault" (پرخاش in a violent sense). خاک شد لاژورد is a simple predication: خاک "dust" شد "became" لاژورد "lapis-lazuli" — a poetic hyperbole saying the dust became blue because of his assault.
segments.s174-g387: زمانی ... زمانی is a correlative adverbial pair meaning "now... now.../at one moment... at another" and expresses alternation. به + instrument (here به خنجر, به گرز) marks the instrument or manner "with/by a dagger / with/by a mace."
segments.s174-g388: همی ریخت is a habitual/progressive past: همی gives continuous aspect ("kept/repeatedly"), ریخت is the past of ریختن meaning "to pour/flow/throw down." آهن is the patient/topic "iron" (poetically: weapons/metal), ز بالای marks origin "from atop," and برز is the high place/height — together: "iron kept pouring down from the high summit."
segments.s174-g389: از آن زخم is an ablative/casual phrase "from/ because of that wound." گوپال is the name associated with زخم ("the wound of Gupāl" or "wound by Gupāl"). گیو دلیر is a nominative/name + epithet construction "Giv the brave," presented after the causal phrase as the salient person in the clause.
segments.s174-g390: سران را: سران ("leaders/heads") is marked by the definite-object/topic marker را. همی شد is the verb phrase "became" with continuous/iterative nuance from همی. سر از جنگ سیر is an idiomatic predicative phrase: "to be sated/full (سیر) of war (جنگ)" with سر serving in the idiom ("their heads became fed up with war"). The object precedes the predicate because of را marking.
segments.s174-g391: دل گیو is a possessive word order common in classical Persian: "Giv's heart." خندان شد is a resultative past "became smiling/gleeful." از زور خشم is a causal phrase where زور ("strength/force") governs خشم ("anger") — "because of the force of his anger." The whole clause states the cause (anger's force) and the resulting inner state (heart smiling).
segments.s174-g392: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for'/'because'). چون + noun (چون چشمه) sets up a simile "like a spring." بودیش is an archaic past form (بود + ـش enclitic = "it was/it belonged"). دریا به چشم is the predicate "a sea to the eye," i.e. figuratively 'a sea in the eye' (an image for abundant tears); the simile links so the clause explains how the feeling appeared to the eye.
segments.s174-g329: ببوسید is a 3rd-person singular past narrative verb 'he kissed'. پیشش is a prepositional/locative clitic meaning 'before her' or 'in her presence'. زمین is the direct object 'the ground', and پهلوان (placed after the verb phrase) is the subject 'the champion/hero' — Persian allows the subject to follow the verb for emphasis or poetic order.
segments.s174-g330: بدو گفت is the light structure 'he said to her' with بدو ('to her') as dative clitic + گفت ('said'); in this gloss گفت is used as the past-tense verb 'said' (the supplied sense list lacks the verbal sense). کای is the vocative particle 'O', مهتر is a vocative noun 'chief/head', and بانوان is the addressee 'ladies' (here 'chief of the ladies').
segments.s174-g331: همه پاسبانیم is a nominal predicate: همه 'all/everyone' + پاسبانیم ('we are guards') where -یم marks 1st-person plural copula. و links clauses. گنج آن تست is a nominal clause 'that treasure is ...' with آن 'that' pointing to the treasure; تست here functions as an archaic/pedestrian copular/predicative element meaning 'is/yours' in the poem (the precise lexical senses in the supplied list do not include this copular use).
segments.s174-g332: فدی کردن جان is an infinitival phrase: فدی ('sacrifice/ransom') + کردن (light verb 'to do/make') taking جان ('life') as its object — 'to offer life as a ransom'. Then و رنج آن تست parallels the previous clause: رنج 'suffering/hardship' with آن 'that' and تست again functioning as the poetic predicative element ('is yours' or 'belongs to you').
segments.s174-g333: زمین از تو گردَد is a causal phrase: از تو 'from/because of you' gives the cause, and گردَد (3rd-person singular of گشت/گردیدن used here as 'become') states the change — 'the earth becomes'. بهار بهشت is a poetic apposition meaning 'the spring of paradise' (here the predicate complement of گردد).
segments.s174-g334: سپهر از تو زاید همی repeats the causal pattern: سپهر 'the sky/heavens', از تو 'because of you', and زاید (3rd-person singular of زادن) 'brings forth/gives birth'. همی is an archaic adverb meaning 'always' or 'continually' placed before the predicate. خوب و زشت are the coordinated results 'the beautiful and the ugly'.
segments.s174-g335: This is an optative/formulaic wish. جهان 'the world' is the subject/topic, پیش فرزند تو 'before/at the presence of your son' is a locative phrase, and بنده باد is the predicate with باد functioning as the optative particle 'may (it be) a servant' (i.e., 'may the world serve/bow before your son').
segments.s174-g336: سر بدسگالانش افگنده باد is another optative clause: سر 'head' is fronted for emphasis, بدسگالانش is a possessive plural 'his/its ill-omened/unlucky ones' (بدسگال + plural/possessive), افگنده is a verbal adjective 'cast down/laid low', and باد again is the optative 'may' — 'may the heads of his ill-omened ones be cast down.'
segments.s174-g249: چنین is a discourse adverb meaning “thus/so” and introduces the reported speech. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (here used elliptically as the reporting verb); شبرنگ is the speaker’s name (a proper noun) and بهزاد را marks the addressee with the definite object/indirect-object marker را (here marking the person to whom the speech is addressed). Word order is Topic (چنین) + reporting verb + indirect object marked with را.
segments.s174-g250: که introduces the quoted clause ('that' / 'namely'). فرمان مبر is a negative imperative: فرمان (command/order) is the noun object and مبر is the prohibitive form of بردن here meaning 'do not take' or 'do not accept'. ز این سپس is an adverbial phrase meaning 'from this time onward / henceforth' (ز + این سپس = 'from now on'). باد را is the noun باد ('wind') with the object/topic marker را; here it functions idiomatically as the source/agent from which one should not take commands ("from the wind" = from a fickle source). Word order is typical older Persian: verb-final prohibition follows its object and adverbials.
segments.s174-g251: همی باش is an imperative built with همی (an older/perfective marker meaning 'still' / continuous sense) + باش (imperative of بودن 'be'), giving the instruction 'remain / be'. بر کوه و در مرغزار are two locative phrases joined by و: بر کوه 'on the mountain' and در مرغزار 'in the meadow'; the prepositions بر and در precede their nouns, a normal Persian order.
segments.s174-g252: چو here means 'when' (introducing a temporal subordinate clause). کیخسرو is the subject (proper name). آید is the present/future-form verb 'comes' (classical narrative often uses the simple present to mean 'will come' or 'comes'). ترا is the enclitic object 'you' (ت) with the object marker را written separately here (ت + را = 'you' as object), and خواستار is an adjective/participle meaning 'seeking' or 'desirous' modifying Kiakhusrow (i.e. 'when Kiakhusrow comes seeking you').
segments.s174-g253: ورا is an older pronominal/locative form meaning 'for him / to him / on his behalf' (marks beneficiary or direction). بارگی ('leafage, foliage') is used metaphorically: 'be (his) foliage'—i.e. give shelter—followed by باش, the imperative 'be.' گیتی بکوب is a second imperative: گیتی 'the world' (direct object) + بکوب 'strike, beat' (imperative). The two imperatives are joined by و.
segments.s174-g254: This clause gives the manner/result: ز دشمن زمین را is a fronted prepositional/partitive phrase 'the earth/from the enemy' (here meaning 'clear the land of the enemy'—ز marks separation or removal). به نعلت is an instrumental phrase 'by/with your horseshoe' (به + نعل + -ت, where -ت is the 2nd‑person suffix attached to نعل 'horseshoe'). بروب is an imperative 'sweep away' (or 'drive off'). The verb follows its object/phrase in the classical word order.
segments.s174-g255: نشست here is a past tense verb meaning 'alighted / sat down / dismounted' (context shows it means dismounted from the horse). از بر اسپ is a classical locative+ablative sequence meaning 'from upon the steed' (از + بر + noun). سالار نیو is a nominal phrase 'the commander Niu' (title + name) in normal modifier order.
segments.s174-g256: پیاده is an adverb/phrase meaning 'on foot'. همی رفت is the narrative/simple past construction: همی (imperfective marker) + رفت (past of رفتن) here narrates 'he went / proceeded.' بر پیش is a prepositional phrase 'in front of / before' and گیو is the proper name 'Giv', the person before/on whose behalf the motion is described. Word order is adverbial + verb + prepositional phrase + proper name.
segments.s174-g49: Word order is object (پسر) + object marker (را) + verb (بفرمود) + agent (گودرز پیر), a common poetic/orderly inversion: literally “son (object) he said Goudarz the old.” بفرمود is an elevated/polite past verb here functioning as “ordered/commanded” (speech verb used for issuing an order).
segments.s174-g50: به توران شدن is an infinitival/nominalized verbal phrase meaning “going/becoming to Turan”; کار را is the topicalized/definite noun “the matter/affair” marked with را; ناگزیر is a predicate adjective “inevitable/compelled.” Note the copula is omitted (typical in poetry) so the clause reads ‘the matter of going to Turan (was) inevitable.’
segments.s174-g51: به فرمان او is an agentive phrase meaning “by his command” (به used adverbially); گیو is the proper name; بسته here is a poetic adjective meaning ‘bound/armed/prepared’ (lit. ‘tied up’); میان functions adverbially as ‘into the midst’ or ‘among them.’ Word order places the adverbial prepositional phrase first, then the named warrior with his descriptive adjective.
segments.s174-g52: بیامد is simple past ‘came’ (here third-person past). به کردار is an idiomatic prepositional phrase ‘in the manner of’ (به used to mark the manner), and شیر ژیان is a figurative expression ‘lion-life’ i.e. ‘lion-like in behavior’ — so the whole phrase means he came behaving/acting like a lion.
segments.s174-g53: همی is an archaic/progressive particle meaning ‘continually/kept on’; تاخت is past tense ‘galloped/rushed’; the clause uses تا … رسید (‘until … reached’) to mark extent: ‘kept riding until he arrived at the Turanian border.’ Note poetic sequence places the verb تاخت before the subordinate phrase.
segments.s174-g54: هر آن کس که is a free relative meaning ‘whomsoever/anyone who’; در راه تنها is a locative phrase ‘alone on the road’; بدید is the past tense ‘saw’ with the archaic verbal prefix ب- (classical past form, equivalent to دید). The clause is a relative clause describing people encountered.
segments.s174-g55: زبان را is the definite/topic-marked object ‘the tongue / speech’; به ترکی here means ‘into/into the Turkish language’ (به used to indicate the language/manner); بیاراستی is an archaic poetic verb meaning roughly ‘set/adjusted’ or ‘made (one’s speech) fit’ — i.e. ‘he made his speech Turkish’ (note: the supplied sense list does not include this verbal sense, so the precise supplied senses do not match the usage).
segments.s174-g56: ز کیخسرو is ‘from Kay Khosrow’ (ز as poetic ‘from’). از وی نشان is ‘a sign/token from him’ (از marking source), and خواستی is second-person past ‘you asked/ requested’ (here narrative past). The clause therefore reports that a request for a sign/token was made to Kay Khosrow concerning that person; word order and the double source-marking are typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s174-g233: یکی is an indefinite determiner ('a certain' / 'one'), followed by the fused predicate مرغزارست (مرغزار + ست), an older poetic copula meaning 'is a meadow'. ز + ایدر is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from here', and نه دور is a negated adjective phrase 'not far' placed after the place phrase; word order is subject-indefiner → predicate → locative phrase → distance qualifier.
segments.s174-g234: به یک‌سو is a directional phrase 'toward one side'; ز here is the poetic 'from/of' combining with راه to give 'from the road'. سواران تور is a genitive grouping 'the horsemen of Tur' (proper name Tour), with the possessor placed after the possessed in a terse poetic ordering (the usual izāfa is omitted).
segments.s174-g235: همان identifies or points to the thing ('that/the very'), followed by the noun جویبار and the copula است 'is'. و links the clause to آب روان, where روان is an adjectival/postpositive attribute 'running' modifying آب ('running water').
segments.s174-g236: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which/that'. از دیدنش is an ablative/'from seeing it' phrase; -ش on دیدن is the clitic object 'it' (referring to the stream). تازه گردد is a present subjunctive/optative-like construction 'becomes fresh', with گردد (گرد +د) used poetically as 'becomes'; روان here is used as a noun 'spirit/soul' (object that is refreshed). Word order is ablative phrase → verbal predicate at end.
segments.s174-g237: تو is an emphatic subject pronoun. بر + گیر functions as a prefixed verb (بر- as verbal prefix + گیر, imperative 'take up'/'seize'); in Persian poetry the prefix is written separately here. زین و لگام are the direct objects ('saddle and bridle'), linked by و, and سیاه is a postpositive adjective 'black' modifying the preceding noun(s).
segments.s174-g238: برو is the simple imperative 'go'. سوی is the preposition 'toward', and آن مرغزاران پگاه is the destination phrase 'those meadows (at) dawn' where پگاه ('dawn/early morning') functions as a temporal/qualifying word associated with the meadows or the time to go. Order is verb → prepositional direction → noun phrase.
segments.s174-g239: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when/as'. The phrase خورشید بر تیغ گنبد شود is a poetic simile: خورشید is the subject, بر تیغ indicates 'upon the sword', and گنبد شود ('becomes a dome') is the verbal predicate placed at the end; the image is that the sun appears like a dome over the swords (a glittering dome).
segments.s174-g240: گه is an adverb 'sometimes/at times'. خواب و خورد are coordinated items meaning 'sleep and mishap/what befalls' (خورد here used in the sense 'befall/happen' poetically). سپهبد is the subject/affected person 'the spahbed (commander)', and شود is the verb 'befall/come about' placed at the end; overall: 'sometimes sleep and misfortune befall the commander.'
segments.s174-g369: سپاهی is a collective noun 'army, troops' (here referring to the assembled force). بر این‌گونه is a prepositional/idiomatic phrase meaning 'in this manner / thus' (بر + این‌گونه). گرد is used adverbially 'around, about' and follows the manner phrase; و جوان is the coordinating conjunction plus adjective/noun 'and young' that connects this descriptive phrase to the following clause. Word order is poetic: modifiers and adverbial phrases follow the noun and prepare the scene for the next verb clause.
segments.s174-g370: برفتند is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person plural verb 'they went / set out.' In Persian poetic word order the verb precedes the explicit subject here; بیدار is a predicative adjective 'awake' placed after the verb, and دو پهلوان is the subject 'two champions' appearing after the verb for emphasis. The whole clause reads 'two champions went forth, awake.'
segments.s174-g371: فرنگیس is the proper name 'Farangis.' با introduces an accompaniment 'with.' رنج‌دیده is a compound adjective 'rājndide' literally 'suffering/afflicted' describing پسر 'son.' The whole NP reads 'Farangis with her suffering/weary son.'
segments.s174-g372: به خواب اندر آورده بودند is a compound verbal phrase: به خواب ('into/to sleep' — به used directionally), اندر آوردن 'to bring into' (here idiomatically 'to lie down / go to sleep'), and بودند is the past auxiliary 'they had' (past perfect/pluperfect sense). سر ('head') is an idiomatic noun meaning 'one's head' and functions as the internal object of the expression (i.e. 'laid their head to sleep' = 'went to sleep'). In context the phrase means 'had laid down to sleep / had gone to sleep.'
segments.s174-g373: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' indicating cause. پیمودن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'traversing / traveling (of),' taking راه 'the road' as its complement. و رنج شبان connects 'and the hardship of the nights / of the journey' (رنج 'hardship, suffering'). This whole phrase gives the causal background ('from the travel of the road and the hardships of the nights'). Note شبان is a poetic form here and its exact nuance ('of the nights' vs. 'youths') depends on context.
segments.s174-g374: جهانجوی is a topicalized noun 'the world‑seeking / ambitious one.' را is the definite object/topic marker (here marking جهانجوی as the person concerned). گیو is the proper name 'Giv.' بد is a predicative adjective 'bad / poor' and پاسبان 'guard' is the predicate noun; the copula 'was' is omitted (a common poetic omission), so the clause means 'Giv was a poor guard for the ambitious man.' Word order (topic marked with را, then subject and predicate) is typical of Persian narrative style.
segments.s174-g375: دو تن is 'two persons' (تن used here as 'person'). خفته is the postpositive adjective/participle 'asleep.' و links to the next phrase: گیو با رنج و خشم means 'and Giv, with suffering/grief and anger' — با introduces the attendant states 'with sorrow and anger.' These coordinated phrases set contrasting states (two asleep vs. Giv alert but grieved and angry).
segments.s174-g376: به راه سواران is a directional/genitive phrase 'toward the road of the riders' (به indicating direction and سواران 'riders' in a genitive relation to راه). نهاده is the past participle/verb 'placed / set' (here 'had fixed/kept'), and دو چشم 'two eyes' is the direct object placed at the end for emphasis — literally 'had set (his) two eyes on the riders' road.' The structure expresses focused watching: Giv had fixed his gaze on the horsemen's path.
segments.s174-g433: همانا که is an emphatic discourse marker: همانا = 'truly/verily' and که introduces the clause that follows. گوپال here is a proper name (or a title treated as a name) and precedes the quantity phrase بیش از هزار ('more than a thousand'), i.e. standard Persian order where the noun comes before its quantifier.
segments.s174-g434: گرفتی is a past tense (perfect/simple past) verb form 'took/seized' (here used transitively). ز دست من is a prepositional phrase 'from my hand' (ز = 'from', دست = 'hand', من = 'my'). آن نامدار at the end appositionally qualifies the subject as 'that famed one' (آن = 'that', نامدار = 'renowned'), a typical poetic placement of an epithet after the verb phrase.
segments.s174-g435: سرش is the noun سر ('head') with the possessive suffix -ش ('his'). ویژه is an adjective/adverb meaning 'special' or 'notable' and here strengthens the statement. گفتی normally would be the 2nd-person past 'you said', but the listed lemma senses are nominal ('conversation'); the function here is verbal ('you said' or 'it is said') introducing the content clause. که introduces the reported clause; سندان شده‌ست uses شده‌ست (compound perfect) meaning 'has become' (سندان = 'anvil').
segments.s174-g436: بر is the preposition 'on/upon' (here linking the comparison), و = 'and'. ساعدش is 'his forearm' (ساعد + -ش). پیل دندان is a compound image: پیل = 'elephant' and دندان = 'tooth/tusk', so 'elephant-tusk' (i.e. tusk-like); شده‌ست is the same perfect copula 'has become'—a poetic simile: his forearm has become like an elephant's tusk.
segments.s174-g437: This clause uses a mixture of word orders: من is the speaker 'I', آورد is the past tense 'brought/carrried out' and رستم is the person named (here as the agent). The phrase بسی دیده‌ام ('I have seen many/much') uses بسی as an indefinite intensifier 'much/many' and دیده‌ام is the perfect 'I have seen'—together indicating the speaker has personally witnessed many deeds by Rostam.
segments.s174-g438: ز + noun is the poetic prepositional form 'from/of': ز جنگ‌آوران = 'from/among the warriors'. نیز = 'also/too'. بشنیده‌ام is the compound present-perfect 'I have heard' (بـ- prefix + شنیده‌ام), indicating hearsay in addition to the speaker's own observation.
segments.s174-g439: به زخمش = 'at/regarding his wound' (به as a prepositional marker + زخمش 'his wound' with possessive -ش). ندیدم is the negated past 'I did not see'. چنین پایدار = 'so/such steadfast' (چنین = 'such/so', پایدار = 'steady/lasting'), giving the sense 'I have not seen such steadfastness when wounded.'
segments.s174-g440: در introduces the domain 'in/throughout' (در کوشش و پیچش کارزار = 'in the effort and writhing of battle'). کوشش = 'effort/struggle', و = 'and', پیچش here carries the sense of bodily twisting or 'writhing' (a common epic image of combat), and کارزار = 'battle' or 'battlefield'. The phrase continues the negation from the previous clause (nor in ...).
segments.s174-g1: بسا is an adverbial quantifier placed before the noun meaning ‘many/often’. رنج‌ها is the plural subject ‘sufferings/hardships’. کز is the classical relative particle ‘that/which’ introducing the clause referring to those hardships. دیده‌اند is 3rd-person plural perfect (they have seen/been seen) used impersonally/poetically to mean ‘are seen/experienced in’ the world.
segments.s174-g2: ز بهر together = ‘for the sake of/for’. بزرگی is the abstract object ‘greatness/grandeur’. پسندیده‌اند is 3rd-person plural perfect of پسندیدن ‘to choose/approve’; here it means ‘they accept/undertake (them) / consider them fitting’ — linking back to the hardships in the previous segment.
segments.s174-g3: سرانجام is an adverb meaning ‘in the end/finally’. بستر is the subject ‘bed’. جز ... نیست is an idiom meaning ‘is nothing but / except’; از خاک (lit. ‘of/from dust’) gives the material — so the clause reads ‘the bed, in the end, is nothing but earth/dust.’
segments.s174-g4: از او = ‘from it / of it’ (referring back to the world/bed). بهره is ‘portion/share’. زهر است = ‘is poison’ (copula + predicate). و تریاک نیست = ‘and there is no opium’ (contrast: there is poison but not the soothing opiate). The clause comments on the quality of that final share.
segments.s174-g5: چو is a classical conjunction here meaning ‘if/since/when’. دانی is 2nd-person singular present ‘you know’. که introduces the subordinate content clause. ایدر = ‘here’. نمانی is 2nd-person present/future ‘you will remain’ (matching دانی). دراز is an adverb ‘long’ modifying نمانی: together ‘you will not remain long here’ (context implies impermanence).
segments.s174-g6: به تارک چرا بر نهی is a rhetorical question: چرا = ‘why’; بر = preposition ‘on/upon’; تاج آز = ‘the crown of greed’. نهی is the 2nd-person present form ‘you place/put’ (classical usage). به تارک (on the top of the head) specifies where the crown is placed. NOTE: the provided sense list for تارک and نهی did not include the head/put senses, so those are treated as irregular/classical usages.
segments.s174-g7: همان = ‘that same/that very’. آز = ‘greed’. را is the definite object marker. زیر خاک = ‘under the earth’ (locative). آوری is 2nd-person present/future of آوردن ‘you bring/bury/put’ (classical usage often reads as ‘you will…’ or as imperative/gnomic statement). The clause is a prediction/imperative about disposing of that greed.
segments.s174-g8: سرش را = ‘its/his head’ (سر + enclitic ش for ‘its/his’), را marks it as object. سر اندر مغاک آوری repeats ‘head’ with the locative اندر مغاک ‘into the abyss/gulf’, then the verb آوری again ‘you bring/cast’. The double mention of سر is emphatic: ‘you will throw its head into the pit.’ آوری is the same 2nd-person present/future form as above.
segments.s174-g9: ترا (ت + را) is the preposed object/beneficiary 'to you' (تو را) with the enclitic object marker را; placing it first gives emphasis. زین is the archaic/poetic form ('ز' + 'این') meaning 'of/from this' (not the saddle sense listed), جهان 'this world/this life' is the domain, شادمانی is the subject 'joy', and بس است is a copular phrase 'is enough' (بس = enough, است = is).
segments.s174-g10: کجا is an interrogative adverb here used rhetorically ('what good/where is...'), رنج تو 'your suffering' uses postposed possessive, بهر functions as 'for/for the sake of', دیگر کس 'another person/anyone else', and است is the copula; the clause asks what use your pain is for someone else.
segments.s174-g11: تو رنجی is a nominal predicate 'you are in pain' without an explicit copula (typical in Persian). و simply links clauses. آسان دگر کس خورد means 'another person experiences ease' — آسان (easy) modifies the clause, دگر کس is the subject 'another/other person', and خورد (from خوردن) here is an intransitive, archaic-use verb meaning 'befalls/experiences' or 'takes/gets' rather than literal 'eat'.
segments.s174-g12: سوی = 'toward'; گور و تابوت = 'grave and coffin' as the direction/goal; تو is the possessive 'your' attached to those nouns; ننگرد (negative + نگرد, 3rd ps. sing.) means 'he does not look/turn (his gaze) toward' — the subject is the 'دگر کس' from the previous clause.
segments.s174-g13: بر او 'upon/to him' marks the experiencer, نیز = 'also', شادی is the subject 'joy', and سرآید (سر آمدن) is a verbal form 'comes/occurs' in the 3rd person; همی is an archaic particle adding emphasis/continuity ('will/does'). The clause states that joy will befall him as well.
segments.s174-g14: سرش is 'his head' (سر + possessive enclitic ش), زیر گرد literally 'under dust' (زیر = under; گرد here = 'dust'), اندر آید is a compound verbal phrase 'comes into / is put into' (اندر = in/into, آید = comes), and همی again adds an archaic emphatic/continuous sense. The whole means his head will be laid under the dust (i.e., he will be buried).
segments.s174-g15: ز is the poetic preposition (from/of = 'about' here), روز گذر کردن is a noun+light-verb construction 'the passing of days' (گذر 'passing (of time)' + کردن as the light verb), and اندیشه کن is the imperative 'think/reflect!'. Together: 'Consider the passing of days.'
segments.s174-g16: پرستیدن is the infinitive 'to worship' used as the object of the imperative that follows. دادگر is 'the Just' (here an epithet, likely of God or a just ruler), پیشه is 'profession/practice', and کن is the imperative 'make/do' — 'make worship of the Just your occupation/practice.'
segments.s174-g209: یکی here is an indefinite 'someone / a certain person' introducing an unnamed agent. تیغ هندی گرفته is a perfect participial clause 'having taken an Indian sword' (گرفته = past participle of گرفتن) that follows the agent; به چنگ is a prepositional phrase 'into/into the grasp' (به + چنگ) describing how the sword was held.
