segments.s174-g210: هر آن کس که ... is a relative construction meaning 'whoever / any person who'; هر = 'whichever/any', آن = definite demonstrative, کس = 'person', که = relative 'who/that'. پیش آمدی is 'came forward / approached' (آمد[ن] 'to come'); بی‌درنگ is an adverb 'immediately' modifying the implied action that follows.
segments.s174-g211: زدی is the past tense verb 'he struck' (classical narrative form). گیو is the proper name (the direct object). بیدار دل is an epithet 'wakeful‑hearted / alert‑hearted' describing Giv. گردنش is گردن + enclitic possessive -ش 'his neck', showing that the neck belongs to Giv (the object).
segments.s174-g212: به زیر گل و خاک is a prepositional locative phrase 'into/under mud and dust' (به + زیر + noun phrase). کردی is the past tense 'you/they made/did' used here as the main verb 'laid / caused to be placed'; تنش = تن + -ش 'his body', the direct object (body of the same person marked by the clitic). Word order places the locative phrase before the verb and the object at the end with the possessive clitic.
segments.s174-g213: برفتند is the past plural 'they went / departed'. سوی سیاووش = 'toward Siavash'. گرد after the phrase functions as a poetic adverb 'around / about' (they went around / surrounded Siavash); placement after the noun phrase is a stylistic/poetic order.
segments.s174-g214: چو آمد begins a temporal clause 'when it came / when (they) came'. دو تن را uses را as a topic/definite marker referring to 'the two people' (the experiencers). دل و هوش گرد is a compact predicate: 'their heart and awareness/wit were turned' — گرد here functions as a verbal/participial element meaning 'became/was turned' (classical usage meaning being struck/confused).
segments.s174-g215: فرنگیس را is the marked direct object ('Farangis'). نیز = 'also'. کردند یار is 'they made (her) a companion' — کرد(ند) + predicative noun یار ('companion/ally'); in Persian ساختار 'کردند یار' = 'made (into) a companion'.
segments.s174-g216: نهانی is an adverb 'secretly'. بر آن = 'upon that / regarding that' (prepositional phrase). بر نهادند is the compound verb 'they set upon / they put in place' (نهادند = placed, set); کار is the object 'work/affair' — together meaning 'they secretly set that affair in motion' or 'they secretly put that plan into effect.' Note the verb follows the prepositional phrase and takes کار as its object.
segments.s174-g129: گره سست شد بر در رنج او is a past, inchoative description: گره (subject) + سست (predicate adjective) + شد (past of شدن 'to become') — 'the knot became loose'. بر در is a prepositional phrase 'on/upon the door', and رنج او is a genitive 'his suffering/anguish' modifying در. Word order places the verb (شد) after the adjective as a light copula/inchoative.
segments.s174-g130: پدید آمد is a verbal phrase 'appeared/came forth' (پدید + آمد). آن is a demonstrative 'that/the' pointing to the noun, نامور is an epithet 'renowned/famed' qualifying گنج, and او is the possessive/pronominal clitic 'his' (the possessor follows the noun). The verb precedes the noun phrase here, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s174-g131: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. کیخسرو is the subject (proper name). از چشمه is a locative/ablative phrase 'from/at the spring' giving place, او را is the direct object with the object marker را (animate personal object), and بدید is the past tense 'saw'. The clause literally reads 'When Kay Khosrow saw him (from/by the) spring...'.
segments.s174-g132: بخندید is simple past 'he laughed'. و links the two actions. شادان is an adverbial/adjectival 'joyful, glad'; دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش for 'his'), and بردمید (here transitive/passive-sense) means 'seized/carry off' — idiomatically 'joy seized his heart' or 'his heart was filled with joy.'
segments.s174-g133: به دل گفت is an idiom 'he said to his heart' = 'he said to himself' (internal thought). کاین is the relative/pointing clause 'that this...', where ک = که ('that') fused with این ('this'). گرد here is an archaic Shahnameh term meaning 'hero/warrior' (not the common modern senses of گرد); جز + [noun] نیست = 'is none other than / is not except'. گیو is the proper name Giv, and نیست is the negative copula 'is not'.
segments.s174-g134: بدین مرز خود is a locative phrase 'in/at this border itself' (بدین = 'by/with/at this', مرز = 'border/realm', خود emphatic 'itself/own'). زین نشان = 'this sign/token' (زین here is a demonstrative/partitive in older usage or could be read as 'this saddle/attire' in literal senses), نیو نیست is negative 'there is no ...' The clause as a whole gives an emphatic negative: 'in this region there is no such mark.'
segments.s174-g135: مرا ... را (here split in the data) is the first-person object 'me' marked by را. کرد is the past 'made/caused' (i.e. 'made me ...'). خواهد is a verbal modal 'wishes/will', همی is an adverbial particle adding emphasis/continuity (poetic), and خواستار is an adjective/noun 'desirous/petitioner' — together the compressed phrase means 'he made me desirous / he made me a suitor/petitioner.' Word order and particles are compacted in poetic diction.
segments.s174-g136: به ایران برد is a purposive phrase 'to carry/bring to Iran' (به ایران = 'to Iran'; برد here is the verb 'to take/bring' in past or infinitive sense in poetic compression). تا introduces a purpose clause 'so that/in order that' and takes the subjunctive کند 'he may do/make'; شهریار is the predicate nominative 'king' — 'to bring (him) to Iran so that (someone) may make him king.'
segments.s174-g201: دل خسرو از درد و رنجش بسوخت is a simple past-tense clause: دل خسرو (Khosrow's heart) is the subject phrase, followed by از + nouns indicating cause (درد و رنجش, 'pain and suffering'). بسوخت is the past passive/intransitive form meaning 'was burned/consumed' and here describes an emotional burning. Word order is subject → cause phrase (introduced by از) → verb, typical of Persian poetic style.
segments.s174-g202: به کردار آتش رخش برفروخت uses به + an instrumental/analogical phrase (به کردار = 'in the manner of' / 'like') to introduce a comparison; کردار here means 'behavior/conduct'. آتش رخش is a genitive construction 'Rakhsh's fire', and برفروخت is a poetic transitive/predicative past meaning 'kindled/was inflamed.' The clause says his conduct flared up, compared to the horse Rakhsh's fire.
segments.s174-g203: بدو گفت کاکنون ز رنج دراز begins with بدو ('to him'), a dative clitic/phrase marking the indirect object. گفت is the reporting verb ('said'), followed by کاکنون ('now, at present') and ز + noun phrase indicating reason or source (ز رنج دراز = 'from/after long suffering'). The sequence is typical: indirect object → verb → temporal marker → causal phrase.
segments.s174-g204: ترا بردهد بخت آرام و ناز is a subordinate clause introduced without an explicit complementizer: ترا (ت + را = 'you' as direct object) marks the beneficiary, بردهد is an older/fixed-form verb meaning 'will give/bestow' (future/optative sense in epic style), and بخت آرام و ناز lists what is given ('fortune/lot' qualified by آرام 'calm' and ناز 'indulgence/tenderness'). Word order: object (beneficiary) → verb → theme (what is given).
segments.s174-g205: مرا چون پدر باش و با کس مگوی is an imperative request: مرا (here split in the data as م + را) is the object 'me', چون پدر باش is an imperative 'be like a father', و connects clauses, and با کس مگوی is a negative imperative 'do not tell anyone.' The clause uses polite/command mood (باش, مگوی) and places the object before the imperative for emphasis.
segments.s174-g206: ببین تا زمانه چه آرد به روی begins with the imperative ببین ('see' / 'wait and see'), then تا introduces a clause of result or temporal waiting ('until/so that'), زمانه چه آرد ('what the times will bring') is the embedded interrogative content, and به روی literally 'to the face' (i.e., 'in one's lot/appearance'). آرد is the present/future-form 'brings.'
segments.s174-g207: سپهبد نشست از بر اسپ گیو has سپهبد as subject ('the spahbed/commander'), نشست as the past tense verb 'sat down/dismounted' (here 'dismounted' or 'alighted'), followed by از بر + noun indicating the thing he left ('from upon the horse of Giv' — از بر اسپ گیو). The prepositional phrase expresses source/position from which he moved.
segments.s174-g208: پیاده همی رفت بر پیش نیو describes motion: پیاده 'on foot', همی (an archaic/adverbial particle marking continuous or simple past action) + رفت 'went', and بر پیش 'toward/in front of' plus نیو (here the noun following 'in front of'). The clause is a simple narrative line: he went on foot, moving ahead of whatever یا whoever نیو denotes.
segments.s174-g321: همان is a demonstrative (= “that/same”) placed before the noun for emphasis. The phrase is a verbless nominal list: گنج (treasure) followed by two coordinated items (گوپال و برگستوان), here presented appositively as contents or named pieces of the treasure. In poetic Persian such noun-phrases often omit a verb and rely on context to supply ‘(was/contained)’.
segments.s174-g322: همان repeats the demonstrative to pick up the next series; the following nouns are coordinated by و (and). The adjective/qualifier گران appears after the final noun, functioning as a postpositive modifier that qualifies the entire coordinated series (i.e. the mace is heavy/valuable). The clause is also verbless, continuing the enumeration.
segments.s174-g323: در گنج is a prepositional phrase ('in/into the treasure'); بگشاد is an archaic/perfective verbal form (prefix b- + root) used here to mean ‘opened’ (classical past). پیش پسر ('before the youth') is a prepositional/locative phrase placed after the verb; the whole segment is a simple clause 'he opened the treasure before the youth.'
segments.s174-g324: This is a compressed descriptive clause: پر از خون (lit. ‘full of blood’) uses پر + از + noun to mean 'covered/filled with.' رخ ('face') names the affected part; از درد خسته جگر is a causal phrase ('heart/jigar weary from pain') where از introduces the cause and خسته is a predicative adjective modifying جگر. The order is characteristic of Persian poetic compression (adjectives and causes follow the nouns they relate to).
segments.s174-g325: چنین گفت is a fixed classical phrase meaning 'thus he said' (چنین = thus, گفت here functions as the past verb ‘said’ in older usage). با introduces the person addressed (با گیو = 'to/with Giv'); کای is a vocative particle ('O') used before the addressee; برده is a past participle ('having borne/taken') modifying رنج to form برده رنج = 'one who has borne hardship.' The whole string is the reporting clause introducing direct speech.
segments.s174-g326: ببین is an imperative ('look'/'see') addressing Giv. تا introduces the content clause that follows ('so that/… — used here to mark the clause expressing what to see'). ز گوهر … ز گنج is a poetic, prepositional frame meaning 'from the jewels … from the treasure'; چه خواهی is the embedded interrogative/content clause ('what you want'), with خواهی as the second-person present/optative verb 'you want/wish.' The overall structure is imperative + content clause: 'look to see what jewel you want from the treasure.'
segments.s174-g327: ز introduces a list item ('from/of'); دینار names the monetary item. وز is a poetic contraction (here representing و + ز = 'and from') used to link parallel prepositional phrases; گوهر شاهوار is a noun (gem) followed by a postpositive adjective شاهوار ('regal/lofty'), so the adjective follows the noun as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s174-g328: Parallel to the previous segment: ز یاقوت ('from rubies') then وز (and from) introduces the next item; تاج گوهر‌نگار is 'a crown that is gem‑adorned' where گوهر‌نگار is a compound adjective ('jeweled') placed after the noun. The whole segment continues the enumerative inventory of treasure.
segments.s174-g217: که is a relative/conjunction 'that' introducing the clause. هر سه literally 'each three' = 'all three' (هر = each/every + numeral phrase). به ... اندر آرند is a periphrastic motion/turning expression: به راه (toward/onto the road) + اندر (into/in) + آرند (3rd person plural present: 'they turn/bring'); روی following the verb is the thing brought/turned (their faces) — so 'آرند روی' = 'they turn their faces'.
segments.s174-g218: نهان is adjectival/adverbial 'hidden' or 'secretly' modifying the action; از marks separation or source ('from'); دلیران is the plural of دلیر 'brave/valiant'; پرخاشجوی is a compound adjective 'combative/aggressive' qualifying those brave ones. Word order is typical poetic: adjective/adverb (نهان) + prepositional phrase (از ...).
segments.s174-g219: فرنگیس گفت is a simple past-reporting phrase 'Farangis said' (گفت is the past of گفتن used here as speech tag). ار is the conditional 'if'; درنگ آوریم is a subordinate clause with آوریم (1st person plural present/subjunctive) 'we bring/produce/cause' used with درنگ 'delay/tarry' to mean 'if we delay' or 'if we tarry'.
segments.s174-g220: This is the result clause: جهان 'the world / this life' + بر 'upon/on' + دل خویش 'our heart' (دل = heart; خویش is formed from خوی + ش, where ش is the possessive clitic 'his/her/our' here agreeing with the speakers) + تنگ آوریم (تنگ = 'narrow', آوریم = 'we make/cause') — literally 'we cause the world to be narrow upon our heart.' The repeated آوریم marks the result ('we shall make...').
segments.s174-g221: از این آگهی is a prepositional phrase 'of/from this news/information' (از = from/of, این = this, آگهی = information/news). یابد is a present/future-oriented form 'finds/comes to know' (3rd person singular) and here places افراسیاب (Afrasiab) after the verb for emphasis: 'Afrasiab learns of this.'
segments.s174-g222: Here two coordinated predicates follow: نسازد به خورد uses نسازد (negative of سازد, 'he does not set/prepare/make') + به خورد ('to eating', خورد = eat) to mean 'he will not take food' or 'he will not eat.' و links the clauses. نیازد به خواب uses نیازد (a separate verb meaning 'need/seek' or 'resort to' in older usage) + به خواب ('to sleep') to indicate he will not sleep/seek sleep. Both verbs are 3rd person singular and describe the consequences of the preceding clause.
segments.s174-g223: بیاید is an optative/potential form 'may come/it may come'. به marks the manner/in the guise of; کردار here means 'behaviour/form/guise' (so 'in the form/behaviour of'); دیو سپید 'white demon' is the noun phrase describing that guise. Together: 'he may come in the form of a white demon.'
segments.s174-g224: دل is the subject 'the heart'; از introduces the object/cause phrase 'از جان شیرین' = 'concerning/regarding the sweet life' (جان شیرین = 'beloved/sweet life'); شود is the copular/auxiliary 'becomes'; ناامید is the predicate adjective 'hopeless/despairing.' Word order places the cause/target (از جان شیرین) before the verb: 'the heart becomes hopeless about the sweet life.'
segments.s174-g97: فرود آمد is a two-word past action (فرود + آمد) meaning 'he came down' / 'he alighted' (verb at the end of the clause is typical). و links this clause to the next action. اسپ را برگذاشت has the object marker را after اسپ ('the steed') and برگذاشت is a past-tense verb 'left (behind)' — so the whole clause means 'he alighted and left the horse (standing).'
segments.s174-g98: بخفت is the past of خفتن 'to sleep' meaning 'he slept / lay down to sleep'. و again connects actions. همی is an old narrative/adverbial particle ('still/continually' or iterative), here giving an ongoing sense. بر دل اندیشه داشت is literal 'he had thought upon (his) heart': بر = 'upon', دل = 'heart', اندیشه = 'thought', and داشت functions as an auxiliary denoting a continuing state ('was having' / 'kept having'), so the phrase means 'his heart was full of thought' or 'he kept thinking in his heart.'
segments.s174-g99: همی گفت is a conventional narrative formula ('he said' with an iterative/narrative particle). مانا is a proper name (the speaker). که introduces the content clause that follows ('that' / 'that which' / 'that ...').
segments.s174-g100: بر here functions as 'upon' / 'against' (a preposition showing affliction). پهلوان is 'champion, hero' and بد modifies it as 'bad/ill‑fated' (an unfavorable or ill‑starred champion). که is a relative 'who', آن = 'that', خواب = 'dream' (here: the dream he had), and دید is the past of 'to see' meaning 'he saw'. Grammatically the relative clause 'که آن خواب دید' specifies 'the champion who saw that dream.'
segments.s174-g101: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'of' (here 'of/Kay Khosrow' = 'trace of Kay Khosrow'). کیخسرو is the proper name (Kay Khosrow). ایدر means 'here'. نبینم is the 1st person negative present/subjunctive form of 'دیدن' used narratively as 'I do not see' (here 'I see not'). نشان is 'sign, trace'. The word order places the negated verb before the nominal object, typical in older Persian.
segments.s174-g102: چه is a rhetorical interrogative 'what' (i.e. 'what can I do / what have I?'). دارم is present 'I have' (here rhetorical/auxiliary sense). همی again gives an ongoing/continuous sense. خویشتن را uses the emphatic/reflexive 'myself' with the object marker را, and کشان is a verbal adjective/participle 'dragging' (‑ing form) used predicatively; together 'I keep dragging myself' or 'I go on dragging myself along.'
segments.s174-g103: کنون‌گر is an older/poetic compound meaning roughly 'now if' / 'now when' (introducing a condition or circumstance). به رزم‌اند places the verb اند ('they are') after the prepositional phrase; به رزم = 'in/at battle' and رزم‌اند is the copula + noun 'are at war' (verb + plural ending). یاران من is the subject 'my companions' appearing after the verb (verb‑subject order is common in poetry).
segments.s174-g104: به بزم means 'at/to the feast/banquet' (به as a locative/into/at). اندرون = 'within / inner (chamber)'. غمگساران من is 'my companions in sorrow' or 'my mourners' (plural noun plus possessive). The whole phrase says that while the companions are at war, in the inner banquet hall his mourners are present.
segments.s174-g257: بدان is the fused prepositional phrase به + آن meaning “toward that”; it introduces the directional target. تند بالا is an adjectival/adverbial phrase ‘steep/rapid/high’ that qualifies the direction. نهادند is the past, 3rd-person plural of نهادن ‘to place/set’ and functions as the main verb; روی (literally ‘face’) is the thing being set/turned (روی نهادن = “to turn one’s face”), placed after the verb as object.
segments.s174-g258: چنان چون here functions as a connective (‘so/for/as’) introducing the reason or circumstance. بود is the past of بودن and links to the nominal predicate مردم چاره‌جوی (‘the people, seekers of a remedy’); syntactically this is a verbless predication rendered with بود. چاره‌جوی is a compound adjective/noun ‘seeker of a remedy/solution’ describing مردم.
segments.s174-g259: فسیله (فَسیله) is an archaic/poetic noun meaning a short pause or respite and begins a temporal clause. چو آمد = 'when it came' (چو used as temporal ‘when’). به + تنگی marks location/condition ‘at/in the narrow place’ (تنگی = narrowness, distress). فراز (‘height/above’) further specifies the place (‘at the high/narrow spot’).
segments.s174-g260: بخوردند is past 3rd-person plural of خوردن and here means ‘they ate/drank’ (i.e., took refreshment). سیراب is a predicate/adjective meaning ‘sated/quenched’. و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and’; گشتند is past 3rd-person plural of گشتن ‘they turned/returned’; باز is an adverb ‘back/again’, completing the action ‘they turned back.’
segments.s174-g261: نگه کرد is past ‘kept watch/looked’; the personal name بهزاد follows the verb (verb–subject order for emphasis). و links clauses. کی را بدید is a rhetorical object question literal ‘whom did he see?’: کی = ‘who’, را marks the direct object, and بدید is past ‘saw’. This structure leads into the reaction described next.
segments.s174-g262: یکی is an indefinite subject ‘one/a certain’. باد سرد is the noun phrase ‘cold wind’. از جگر is an ablative phrase ‘from the liver/heart’ (جگر here = seat of feeling). برکشید is the past, 3rd-person singular of برکشیدن ‘to lift/draw up’ used figuratively ‘rose up’ — the clause describes a sudden chill or inward fear.
segments.s174-g263: بدید is the past verb ‘saw’. آن نشست functions as the direct object ‘that sitting/assembly’ or ‘that scene’. سیاوش پلنگ is an appositive: the name Sîyâvash followed by the epithet پلنگ (‘panther’), postposed to characterize him.
segments.s174-g264: رکیب here denotes the rider/companion (appositive to Sîyâvash); دراز is an attributive adjective ‘tall/long’. و is the conjunction ‘and’. جناغ خدنگ is a compact descriptive phrase: جناغ ‘breast/sternum’ with خدنگ ‘dagger/short sword’ — together implying a dagger at the chest or a well-armed breast; the nouns are placed after the name as pictorial epithets.
segments.s174-g121: تو گفتی is a simple past addressed to second person: تو (you) + گفتی (you said). The phrase names the topic reported: منوچهر is the subject of the reported clause and بر تخت عاج is a locative prepositional phrase (بر = on) modifying where he sits; عاج (ivory) qualifies تخت (throne).
segments.s174-g122: نشسته‌ست is the archaic/poetic 3rd-person singular stative ('is seated'), here describing Manuchehr. بر سر = 'upon (his) head' (بر + سر), ز is the poetic genitive/partitive 'of/from' that links the material/quality (پیروزه) to تاج (crown). The word order places the verb phrase then its locative/result phrase.
segments.s174-g123: همی ... آمد is a poetic iterative or presentive construction meaning 'there came' or 'kept coming'; همی adds continuity or vividness. بوی مهر is a genitive phrase ('scent of love') where مهر (love/affection) qualifies بوی (scent). از روی او gives the source ('from his face').
segments.s174-g124: Again همی آمد gives a poetic 'there came/issued' sense. زیب تاج is a noun‑noun grouping where زیب (adornment/ornament) qualifies تاج (the crown) — 'the crown's beauty/ornament' — and از موی او states the source ('from his hair').
segments.s174-g125: به دل گفت گیو is the idiom 'Giv said to his heart' = 'Giv thought/remarked to himself'; به دل is an internal dative/locative. این به جز شاه نیست is a verbless/existential style reversed into Persian negative structure: 'this is not anything except a king' (i.e., 'this can only be a king'), with نیست as the negative copula.
segments.s174-g126: چنین چهره is a fronted noun phrase ('such a face'). The pattern جز در X نیست = 'is found only in X' (literally 'except in X it is not'), so جز + در + خور + گاه (here خور = 'sun', گاه = 'place/time' used poetically) yields 'only in the sun's place' — a poetic image for the royal/highest seat. نیست closes the clause.
segments.s174-g127: پیاده here is adverbial/participial ('on foot' or 'dismounted'), بدو = 'to him/toward him', and تیز بنهاد is an archaic past‑form (ب[n]هاد from نهادن) meaning 'set/laid (swiftly)' — together تیز بنهاد روی 'he quickly set/turned his face' (i.e., he turned quickly toward him). روی is the object ('face/toward').
segments.s174-g128: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'); تنگ اندر آمد = 'came in/approached closely' (تنگ = near/narrow, اندر آمد = entered/arrived). گو is the verb 'say' used here as a quotative/performative ('he said'), followed by the vocative شاه‌جوی ('O seeker of kings' / 'aspirant to kingship').
segments.s174-g169: "که" is the relative conjunction 'which/that' introducing a clause; the nominal predicate "میراث بود" is a past-state construction ('was an inheritance'), and the prepositional phrase "از گه کیقباد" ('from the time/occasion of Kay Qobad') locates that inheritance in time. Word order is typical classical Persian (relative + predicate + temporal phrase).
segments.s174-g170: This is a nominal clause without an explicit copula: "درستی" (rightness/sovereignty) is the substantive; "بدان" is the compound preposition "ب(ه) + آن" ('to/for that') here written split, pointing the right toward something; "بد کیان را نژاد" is a noun phrase 'the ill‑born line/race of the Kians' with "را" marking it as the definite/particular bearer of the right. In other words, 'the right belonged to that ill‑born Kian lineage.'
segments.s174-g171: "چو" is the conjunction 'when'; the clause follows SOV order: "گیو (subject) آن نشان (object) دید (verb)". The phrase "بردش نماز" uses the verb "نماز بردن" (to offer/perform prayer) with the enclitic "ش(=آن)" referring back to 'that sign'—literally 'he offered prayer to/for it.'
segments.s174-g172: The particle "همی" marks continuous or habitual aspect in older Persian (here progressive/past-continuous). "ریخت آب" is a poetic idiom ('water poured') meaning tears flowed; the coordinating "و" links the clauses, and "همی گفت راز" ('and he was saying/spoke a secret') places "راز" as the object of "گفت". Note the slightly inverted/poetic order and the archaic aspect marker "همی".
segments.s174-g173: "گرفتش" is a past-tense verb with the enclitic object "ش" ('he took/seized him'); the prepositional phrase "به بر" ('to the bosom/into the arms') follows and "شهریار زمین" is an epithet 'the king/souvereign of the land' serving as agent. The clause reads literally 'the king of the land took him to his breast.'
segments.s174-g174: "ز شادی" ('from/with joy') gives the cause; "بر او" ('over him') is a locative/target phrase; the compound verbal idea "بر گرفت آفرین" literally 'took up (lit. 'seized up') a praise' means he uttered an acclamation. "آفرین" here is an interjection/praise word ('bravo'/'well done').
segments.s174-g175: "از ایران بپرسید" is an instruction/imperative-like plural/formal construction ('ask of Iran' / 'inquire among the Iranians'); "بپرسید" here is the (classical) present/subjunctive/imperative form of پرسیدن. The coordination "و ز تخت و گاه" uses poetic "ز" (= از) to add 'from the throne and the court/seat' as further loci of inquiry.
segments.s174-g176: This continues the list of sources introduced by the poetic preposition "ز" ('from'): "ز گودرز" ('from Goudarz') and then "وز رستم نیک‌خواه" is a contracted sequence (written here together) meaning 'and from Rostam the well-wisher'—that is, ask Goudarz and ask Rostam (who is described as نیک‌خواه).
segments.s174-g73: چنین داد پاسخ که is a formulaic reporting phrase: چنین (thus) + داد (past tense 'gave') + پاسخ (object 'an answer'), with که introducing the content clause. داد is the simple past; که begins the reported statement 'that/which'.
segments.s174-g74: ولیکن introduces a contrast ('but'). پراگنده is a predicate adjective 'scattered/widespread' followed by the copula است. با هر کس is a prepositional phrase 'with/among every person' (هر کس = each person), so the clause says the quality is distributed among people. Word order places the adjective before the copula, as normal.
segments.s174-g75: اگر ... هست آگهی is a conditional clause: اگر (if) introduces the condition; زآن که پرسیم is a relative/subordinate clause 'about that which we ask' (پرسیم here is the 1pl present/subjunctive form 'we ask' used to form the relative). هست is the copula 'there is' and آگهی = 'information'.
segments.s174-g76: ز پاسخ زبان را نیابی تهی is the apodosis (result): ز (from/of) پاسخ 'from the answer', زبان را (the tongue — marked by object marker را) is the theme, نیابی is the negative present/near-future second-person form 'you will not find' (ن- negative + یابی), and تهی 'empty' is predicated of زبان. The clause literally says 'you will not find the tongue empty from the answer.'
segments.s174-g77: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = ب + او = 'to him/unto him', گفت = 'said' in the past). What follows (کیخسرو اکنون کجاست) is direct address plus a question 'Kay Khosrow, where are (you) now?'; کجا + ست forms the interrogative 'where is (he/you)'.
segments.s174-g78: بباید is a modal 'it is necessary / you must'. It takes an infinitival/masdar phrase: برگشادنت is برگشادَن (to open/reveal) with the enclitic -ت marking the second person (literally 'your opening' → 'that you open/reveal'). به من marks the beneficiary/goal 'to me', and راست functions adverbially 'truly/plainly' modifying the revelation.
segments.s174-g79: Again, such a reporting frame: چنین داد پاسخ که ('he answered thus, that') followed by نشنیده‌ام, perfect/experiential first-person 'I have not heard' (ن- negative + شنیده‌ام). Here it means 'I have not heard of (this).'
segments.s174-g80: چنین نام هرگز نپرسیده‌ام: چنین نام 'such a name' is the object; هرگز 'ever/never' and نپرسیده‌ام is the negative perfect first-person 'I have not asked'. The clause states 'I have never inquired about such a name.'
segments.s174-g297: کنون is an initial time adverb ('now'). رفت is simple past (3sg) 'he/it went/left'. The coordination و links that clause to the next clause; رنج مرا is the object phrase ('my suffering') where the pronoun مرا is split in the text into م + را; باد here is the noun 'wind' acting as the subject of کرد, and کرد is the past tense of کردن ('did/caused') so the phrase means literally 'the wind did (to) my suffering' = 'the wind carried off my suffering.' Note the poetic order: the object (رنج مرا) precedes the subject (باد) before the verb کرد.
segments.s174-g298: دل شاد من places the possessor pronoun من after the noun (poetic order) to mean 'my glad heart.' سخت is an adverbial/intensifying word 'greatly/strongly' modifying the adjective ناشاد ('unhappy'). کرد is again past tense 'he/it made/caused', so the clause reads 'he made my glad heart very unhappy.'
segments.s174-g299: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; اسپ is 'horse/steed'; اندر آمد is a two‑word verbal phrase ('came in/entered' or here 'alighted/dismounted'). جهان‌دیده is an epithet 'world‑wise / experienced' placed before the proper name گیو. The overall order is prepositional phrase + verb, with the descriptive adjective preceding the name in classical style.
segments.s174-g300: همی is a classical adverbial particle (here emphatic/continuous/attentive). آفرین functions as a noun/acclamation (the name of the piece or exclamation) and خواند is past tense 'he sang/played/performed' (here 'performed' on an instrument). بر شاه is a dative/toward phrase 'to/for the king', and نیو is the direct object 'the ney (reed‑flute)'. The clause thus means 'he performed the Afarin on the ney for the king.'
segments.s174-g301: که here introduces a benedictory/wish clause ('that/may'). روز و شبان is a paired time expression 'day and night' (classical pairing; شبان is archaic here for nights), and بر تو is a dative 'for you'. فرخنده is the adjective 'auspicious/fortunate' and باد is the optative particle 'may it be' (classical wish construction).
segments.s174-g302: سر is 'head(s)' and بدسگالان is 'the ill‑omened / ill‑fated ones'. The pronoun تو follows as agent/actor in classical word order, and کنده is a past participle 'torn/uprooted'; with the optative باد ('may it be'), the clause expresses a wish: 'may you tear off the heads of the ill‑omened.' Note the participle + باد pattern for a hoped‑for result.
segments.s174-g303: که again introduces a relative/clausal link 'who/that'. با introduces an instrumental/listing phrase 'with' and the nouns برز, اورندی, رای and فر are enumerated attributes ('exaltation/high rank, prowess, counsel/wisdom, and royal glory') describing what accompanies the person addressed. Classical Persian often strings such abstract nouns after با.
segments.s174-g304: ترا is the object 'to you' (written split as ت + را in the manuscript), داد is the past tense 'gave/bestowed', and داور is the agent 'the Judge' (here the divine/judicial figure who grants favors). هنر با گهر is the thing given: 'excellence/virtue (هنر) with/alongside a gem (گهر)'. Word order is somewhat flexible/classical: object (ترا) appears before the verb داد, and the agent داور follows, forming 'to you gave the Judge ...'.
segments.s174-g177: بدو is the prepositional clitic 'to him' placed before the verb for emphasis; گفت is the past‑tense verb 'said' (3rd person). The order بدو گفت گیو is the common Persian inversion 'to‑him said Giv' = 'Giv said to him.' گیو‌ای is the name Giv with the vocative particle ای attached (addressing him). کی is an interrogative/relative particle here meaning 'who' (introducing the description that follows).
segments.s174-g178: This is a string of adjectival epithets modifying the vocative 'جهاندار': سرافراز 'honored/proud', بیدار 'awake/alert', فرخنده 'auspicious/fortunate'. The conjunction و simply joins them; پی follows as a short adverbial/relational particle used poetically ('following/ever' or 'on the watch'), intensifying the description. The whole segment is a direct adjectival address.
segments.s174-g179: جهاندار is repeated as vocative 'O sovereign'; دارنده is a present‑participle adjective 'possessing/holder' that modifies it. خوب و زشت ('good and bad') are the nouns possessed; the structure means 'the sovereign who holds/controls good and bad' or more loosely 'possessor of good and evil.'
segments.s174-g180: مرا‌گر is an inversion for poetic meter equivalent to اگر مرا 'if to me' (= 'if you had shown me'); نمودی is 2nd person past 'you showed' (نمود + ی). سراسر بهشت is the direct object placed after the verb and means 'the whole of Paradise' (سراسر 'throughout/entire', بهشت 'paradise'). The clause is conditional: 'if you had shown me all of Paradise...'.
segments.s174-g181: همان هفت کشور is a noun phrase 'those seven countries/realms' (همان 'those/that', هفت کشور 'seven realms'). به شاهنشهی is a prepositional phrase 'into/under kingship/imperial rule' that indicates the state into which those realms were placed.
segments.s174-g182: نهاده is a past participle/verb meaning 'placed/placed into/ bestow(ed)' and here carries the sense of having conferred or set (note: could be active 'you had placed' or passive/nominal depending on context). بزرگی و تاج مهی are the things bestowed: بزرگی 'greatness' and تاج 'crown' qualified by مهی 'moon‑like' (a poetic epithet meaning shining/beautiful). The coordination ties the gifts together.
segments.s174-g183: نبودی is negative past ('would not have been' / 'was not') formed by ن + بودی; here it gives a counterfactual or emphatic past sense. دل من 'my heart' is the subject/possessed noun phrase. بدین خرمی (بدین = 'in/with this', خرمی = 'joy/delight') is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in such joy' or 'so joyful'. Together: 'My heart would not have been so joyful.'
segments.s174-g184: که here introduces a subordinate clause with causal/relative force ('for/since/that'). روی تو دیدم is a straightforward verbal clause 'I saw your face' (روی 'face' as the object, تو 'you', دیدم 'I saw'); Persian poetry often omits the object marker را. به توران زمی is a locative phrase 'on the soil/land of Turan' (به + place, زمی 'earthly/ground'), giving the place where the face was seen. The clause gives the reason or circumstance for the delight.
segments.s174-g361: ز گردان گزین کرد کلباد را is a compact Persian clause with the preposition ز (from) fronting the source (گردان, “the ranks/troops”), followed by a light verb construction گزین کرد (chose/made a selection). کلباد را is the definite object marked by را. The verb کرد is the past action 'did/selected'; the word order (prepositional phrase → verb → object with را) is typical of classical Persian narration.
segments.s174-g362: چو نستیهن و گرد پولاد را is elliptical: چو here means 'as/like' and introduces two additional names as parallels to the previous object (i.e. 'as Nastīhan and Gerd Polād'). The conjunction و links the two names; the final را marks them as objects in the same sense as کلباد را. Note that گرد here functions as part of a compound name (Gerd Polād) rather than its usual lexical senses.
segments.s174-g363: بفرمود تا ترک سیصد سوار is a reported‑speech / command clause: بفرمود (honorific 'he said/commanded') introduces a subordinate clause with تا meaning 'so that / in order that' and then the direct object/goal 'ترک سیصد سوار' (three hundred Turk horsemen). In classical Persian تا frequently introduces the content of an order ('he commanded that...').
segments.s174-g364: برفتند تازان بر آن کارزار is a main clause describing the result: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they went/advanced', تازان (literally 'raiders' or 'the fierce ones') is the subject, and بر آن کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'to/toward that battle' where بر takes the sense 'toward/onto' and آن specifies 'that battlefield'. Word order is subject → verb → directional phrase, common in older Persian narrative.
segments.s174-g365: سر گیو بر نیزه سازید گفت is a sequence of actions: سر گیو ('Giv's head') is the direct object, بر نیزه سازید is a causative/perfective phrase literally 'made/placed on a spear' (سازید = made/placed), and گفت follows as the speech marker 'said'. In classical narrative, short clauses like this stack actions; سازید is an archaic past form meaning 'set/placed' and گفت functions to introduce ensuing speech.
segments.s174-g366: فرنگیس را خاک باید نهفت is a verbal necessity construction: فرنگیس را (Farangis as definite object) + باید ('it is necessary' / 'must') + نهفت (an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'to hide/bury'). The literal structure is 'Farangis must be hidden by earth' = 'Farangis must be buried.' Note the passive/necessitative sense conveyed by باید + verb.
segments.s174-g367: ببندید کیخسرو شوم را is an imperative/command clause: ببندید (plural imperative or narrative perfect of 'bind/secure') is directed at agents to bind, کیخسرو شوم را is the definite object where کیخسرو is modified by شوم ('ill‑omened'). The را marks the object. The phrase literally instructs 'Bind the ill‑omened Kiyakhshrow.'
segments.s174-g368: بد اختر پی او بر و بوم را is a descriptive clause: بد اختر 'an ill/fateful star', پی او 'following him' (پی = after/following), and بر و بوم را ('upon the land/home' with را marking the definite locus). The sequence states that a bad star follows him over the country; word order places the qualifier (بد اختر) first, then the follower phrase, then the affected domain.
segments.s174-g25: نشاید is an impersonal/formal negative statement meaning “it is not fitting” and heads the clause. خور و خواب are two nouns standing for the actions “eating and sleeping” (a fixed paired expression), and با آن نشست literally “with that نشست” means “in that company/assembly” (نشست here = “gathering, company”). The word order places the negative predicator before the action-noun phrase and the locative/companionship expression (با آن نشست).
segments.s174-g26: The initial که introduces a relative/subordinate clause modifying آن (that company). خستو نباشد is a negative present-subjunctive/optative idea “is not/should not be (in the state of) خستو”; به یزدان marks the relation/target “toward God,” and the final که هست is a parenthetical relative “who/that exists,” with هست serving as the copula meaning “exists/is.”
segments.s174-g27: This is two short predicative phrases joined by juxtaposition: دلش کور باشد = “his heart be blind” uses باشد in the optative/subjunctive sense (classical Persian allows this as a hortative/conditional flavor), and سرش بی‌خرد = “his head [be] foolish” omits an explicit copula but is parallel in function. The enclitic ـش marks possession (“his”).
segments.s174-g28: خردمندش is topical/subject-like (“his wise one” or “his wisdom/wise man”), از مردمان is a prepositional/partitive phrase “from/among men,” and نشمرد is the verb at the end (negative form) meaning “(will) not count/consider.” The verb-final order (topic — adjunct — verb) is typical of poetic/classical phrasing.
segments.s174-g29: ز هستی is an ablative/causal prepositional phrase “from/existence,” followed by نشان است where نشان is the predicate noun “a sign” and است is the copula “is/there is.” بر آب و خاک is a locative phrase “upon water and earth”; word order places the copula after the predicate noun in a slightly inverted, poetic order.
segments.s174-g30: ز دانش is a prepositional/causal phrase “for the sake of / because of knowledge,” منش را is the object with definite marker را (“your character/way”), and مکن is the negative imperative “do not (do).” در مغاک is the locative complement “into the abyss.” The clause literally orders: “Do not make/put your character into the abyss for the sake of knowledge.”
segments.s174-g31: توانا و دانا و دارنده is a coordinated list of predicate adjectives/nouns (“Able, Wise, Possessor”). The sequence is followed by اوست, where او (he) is the subject and ست (a poetic/contracted form of است) is the copula; predicate precedes subject here, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s174-g32: خرد را و جان را are two definite objects marked with را (“wisdom” and “soul/life”), followed by نگارنده اوست where نگارنده is an agent noun “inscriber/writer” and اوست repeats the subject+copula (“he is”). The construction says literally “He is the inscriber of wisdom and of the soul,” with objects fronted before the predicate noun.
segments.s174-g113: سرش is سر + enclitic ش (his), so 'his head.' پر ز غم is a fixed poetic sequence meaning 'full of grief' (پر + preposition ز + noun). گرد آن مرغزار is a circumpositional phrase: گرد here means 'around/about' and takes آن مرغزار ('that meadow') as its complement; the word order is compacted for poetic rhythm.
segments.s174-g114: همی گشت is the imperfective/continuous past sense ('kept roaming' / 'was turning about') with همی marking ongoing action. شه را is marked by the object marker را ('the king' as the thing sought). کنان is an adverbial/participle ('doing so' / 'thus'), and خواستار is a predicative adjective/participle meaning 'desirous/seeking' — together they form 'kept roaming, seeking the king.'
segments.s174-g115: یکی here is the indefinite enumerator 'one / a certain.' چشمه‌ای (with the -ی indefinite) is the direct object, and دید is the simple past 'saw.' تابان is a descriptive adjective placed after the verb phrase ('shining'), and ز دور is a locative phrase 'from afar.'
segments.s174-g116: The second item of the triad begins again with یکی 'one (another).' سرو بالا is a noun + postpositive adjective form ('a tall/lofty cypress'), and دل آرام پور is a noun phrase 'a calm-hearted son/young man' (دل آرام = 'calm-hearted' modifying پور 'son/descendant' used as 'youth' here). The word order (noun + adjective, adjective phrase after noun) is typical in Ferdowsi's verse.
segments.s174-g117: یکی 'one' introduces another figure. جام پر is 'a full cup' (noun + adjective), and می‌گرفته is the imperfective/habitual past form 'was holding / used to hold' (mi‑ prefix + گرفته). به چنگ functions as an instrumental/directional phrase meaning 'in (his) grasp / in the hand' (literal 'to/into the grip').
segments.s174-g118: به سر بر زده is a poetic verbal phrase: بر زده ('struck/upon placed') with به سر ('to/on the head') giving the location — together 'placed on his head.' دسته بوی و رنگ is a noun phrase 'a bundle/plume of scent and color' (دسته 'bundle/tuft' modified by the coordinated nouns بوی و رنگ). The clause is elliptic: the verb 'was placed' is expressed in بر زده and governs دسته.
segments.s174-g119: ز بالای او is a prepositional phrase 'from above him' (ز = 'from', بالای = 'above / atop', او = 'him'). فره ایزدی is a compound noun meaning 'divine far̄r / heavenly royal glory' (فره 'farr' = divine royal splendor, ایزدی = 'divine'). The noun phrase is positioned to be the subject of the following verb.
segments.s174-g120: پدید آمد is a two-word past verb 'appeared' (پدید = 'appear/manifest' + آمد = 'came' used as auxiliary). The coordinating و links the next nominal phrase, رایت بخردی ('banner of wisdom'); the verb is understood to apply to both nouns (i.e., the banner also appeared). بخردی is a genitival/adjectival form 'of wisdom' modifying رایت.
segments.s174-g17: بترس is an imperative (2nd-person) telling the addressee to 'fear'. از + خدا is the usual prepositional phrase 'fear of/from God' (poetic use of از). و links the two imperatives; میازار is a negative imperative 'do not harm/torment' and کس is its object 'anyone / any person'. Together: 'Fear God and do not harm anyone.'
segments.s174-g18: ره رستگاری is a noun phrase 'the way / path of salvation' (ره + رستگاری). همین است و بس is a short emphatic clause: همین است 'this is (it)' and بس 'enough/that's all' — a fixed idiomatic ender meaning 'this alone is the way to salvation.'
segments.s174-g19: کنون‌ای is a vocative/time phrase 'now, O...' (کنون + vocative ای). خردمند بیدار دل is a stacked noun phrase addressing the addressee: خردمند 'wise one', بیدار 'awake/alert', followed by دل 'heart' used figuratively ('O wise, wakeful heart'). The epithets precede the head noun in poetic order.
segments.s174-g20: مشو در گمان is a negative imperative construction: مشو (do not become / do not be) + در گمان 'in doubt/conjecture'. The second clause is elliptical: پای درکش ز گل — درکش here is a verb with the clitic -ش 'it' meaning 'comprehend/realize it' (understand it); پای 'foot/footing' is the topic and ز گل 'from clay' uses poetic ز = از. So the line says: 'Do not be in doubt; understand that your footing comes from clay.'
segments.s174-g21: ترا here is the older split form ت + را (object/indirect object marking) meaning 'to you' / 'your'. کردگار است پروردگار is an inverted copular clause: 'the Maker/Creator is (your) Sustainer/Lord.' The inversion (predicate before nominative) is a common poetic order for emphasis.
segments.s174-g22: توی بنده is a terse phrase meaning 'you are among (the) servants' or simply 'you are a servant' (توی = 'in/among' used idiomatically). و کرده کردگار links the clause: کرده (past participle / 'done, made') + کردگار (the Maker) — here meaning 'and the Maker has done (it)' or 'and the Maker is the doer', so the sense is that you are a servant while the Creator is the active agent.
segments.s174-g23: چو here functions as 'when' (temporal subordinating conjunction). گردن به اندیشه زیر آوری is a causative/expressive clause: گردن 'neck' + به اندیشه 'to/for thought' + زیر آوری (زیر آوردن) literally 'bring under' — i.e., 'bow/submit your neck to reflection' or 'submit yourself to thought.' The verb آوری is 2nd-person present/imperative-like 'you bring/you put'.
segments.s174-g24: ز هستی مکن پرسش و داوری: ز (poetic از) 'about/from' + هستی 'existence/being'. مکن پرسش و داوری is an admonition with negative imperative مکن 'do not' followed by two nouns used as verbal objects: پرسش 'questioning' and داوری 'judging' — 'do not question or judge concerning existence.'
segments.s174-g137: چو آمد = a temporal clause introd. by چو “when” + آمد (past of آمدن) meaning “came.” برش is an older/poetic locative/pronominal form here (“to/at him”), which is elliptical and not one of the literal senses listed; نماز بردن is a fixed poetic phrase meaning “to make obeisance / prostrate in greeting.” Word order is typical older Persian: temporal marker (چو آمد) + complement (برش) + subject/name (گیو) implied, then the verbal phrase (بردش نماز = performed obeisance).
segments.s174-g138: بدو گفت = بدو “to him/unto him” + گفت (past “said”) — the verb is used in narrative past; the listed noun senses for گفت do not match the verbal use, so mapped as -1. کای is the vocative particle “O” addressing someone; نامور سرفراز are two epithets (adjectives) in apposition, “renowned / famed” and “victorious / proud.”
segments.s174-g139: بر آنم که is an idiomatic clause meaning “I am of the mind / I am resolved that …”; بر and آنم here form that fixed expression rather than their literal senses, and توی is an archaic/poetic form of “thou art / you are” (not the locative listed). پور سیاوش is the noun phrase “the son/descendant of Siyâvash.” The relative/complementizer که introduces the clause.
segments.s174-g140: ز تخم کیانی و کیخسروی = a genitive phrase giving lineage: ز (poetic “from”) + تخم “seed/stock” + کیانی “Kayanian (of the Kayan dynasty)” + و “and” + کیخسروی (here ‘of Keykhosrow’ / adjectival epithet). This specifies the pedigree introduced in the previous clause.
segments.s174-g141: چنین داد پاسخ ورا = چنین “thus/so” + داد (past of دادن, “gave”) + پاسخ “answer” + ورا (archaic pronominal allomorph = ‘to him’). Word order is SOV in narrative: adverbial (چنین) + verb (داد) + object (پاسخ) + indirect object (ورا) + speaker (شهریار) follows as subject/title.
segments.s174-g142: که introduces the content of the reply (“that …”). تو گیو گودرزی‌ای نامدار = a nominal clause: تو “you” (address), گیو (proper name), گودرزی‌ای a patronymic/adjectival form meaning “of Goudarz / Goudarzi,” and نامدار “renowned.” The clause is a declarative identification (that you are X).
segments.s174-g143: بدو گفت = “he said to him” (بدو = to him; گفت = said — verbal use not reflected by the noun senses listed). گیو‌ای is the speaker’s name in vocative/identifying position. سر راستان is an epithet: سر here functions figuratively as ‘chief/head/leader’ or honorific and راستان means ‘upright/righteous’; together an address meaning roughly “O upright chief” or “O true one.”
segments.s174-g144: ز گودرز با تو که زد داستان = a compact clause of identification and reference: ز گودرز “of / from Goudarz” (identifying lineage), با تو “with you / as for you” (linking to the addressee), که introduces a relative clause, and زد داستان “struck/uttered the tale” where زد here has the poetic sense “to perform/sound/recite” and داستان means “story/tale.” Together it conveys Giv identifying himself (of Goudarz) and referring to the addressee as the one who related the story.
segments.s174-g81: چو here is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” پاسخ چنین یافت is a compact literary clause: یافت is a past tense verb (3rd person) meaning “found/obtained/received,” with پاسخ (‘answer’) as its object and چنین qualifying that answer (“such/like this”). از رهنمون is a prepositional phrase giving the source (“from the guide/guidance”); note رهنمون is a noun and از marks origin.
segments.s174-g82: This is two coordinated actions. بزد is a past-tense verb “he struck/smote.” تیغ follows as the instrument/object (‘with a sword’—classical Persian often omits a preposition like با). و links to the next verb: انداختش is the verb انداخت + enclitic personal pronoun ـش “him/it” meaning “he cast/threw him,” and سرنگون is a predicative/adverbial adjective “down/overthrown,” describing the result.
segments.s174-g83: به توران همی رفت is a simple past narrative: به marks direction (“to Turan”), همی is the archaic/progressive particle used with the past verb رفت to give a continuous or habitual sense (“was going/went”), and رفت is the past of رفتن. چون here means “as/like,” introducing the comparative phrase بیهشان: بیه is an archaic negative/privative element meaning “without” and the enclitic ـشان “them/their” attaches to it, so چون بیهشان = “as if without them.”
segments.s174-g84: مگر here functions idiomatically to introduce a proviso/hope — roughly “(unless / but rather / perhaps)” and leads into a clause expressing purpose/expectation. یابد is the 3rd person form of یافتن (“find”) and here carries the sense of potential/subjunctive possibility (“may/might find”); از شاه جایی نشان is a noun phrase with از marking source (‘from the king’) and جایی نشان = “some sign/place-mark” (جایی = ‘somewhere/any place,’ نشان = ‘sign/token’).
segments.s174-g85: چنین تا introduces a temporal clause “thus until ….” تا here means “until.” برآمد is a past verb here meaning “arose/occurred/was completed,” used to indicate the lapse of time (“until … had come/elapsed”). بر این هفت سال is the temporal phrase “upon these seven years” (بر = on/upon, این = this/these, سال = year/years), so the whole segment means roughly “thus until these seven years had come/passed.”
segments.s174-g86: میان introduces a locative or circumstantial phrase “amid/in the midst of.” سوده here is presented as a proper name (an archaic/personal name) in this context. از marks the agents/causes “from/by” and links the following hardships: تیغ (“sword”) and بند دوال (“the bond/fetter of twofold”); بند means “fetter/bond” and دوال qualifies it as twofold/double. The phrase describes being caught between sword and double fetters.
segments.s174-g87: This noun phrase lists provisions and clothing: خورش گور = “stew of gūr.” Here گور is the wild ass/onager (not ‘grave’), so خورش گور = onager stew. و connects, پوشش هم از چرم گور = “and a covering/cloak also made from onager leather” (پوشش = covering/cloak, هم = also, از = from, چرم = leather, گور = onager). Word order is noun + material/source marked by از.
segments.s174-g88: گیا (plants/herbage) plus خوردن (the verbal noun/infinitive “to eat” or the gerundial notion “eating”) indicates they subsisted on herbs (“eating plants”). باره here functions as an adverbial/occasional noun (“occasion/for a time” or roughly “for food” in context). و joins the next item: آب شور = “salt/brackish water” (آب = water, شور = saltiness/brine). Overall: they ate plants and drank brackish water.
segments.s174-g65: همی before the verb marks a continuous/habitual action in older Persian; رفت is the past/past-continuous verb 'went/was going'. بیدار follows the verb as a predicative/adverbial adjective meaning 'awake/alert'. با او به راه is a prepositional phrase 'with him on the road' (با = 'with', او = indirect object, به راه = 'along/on the road').
segments.s174-g66: راز is the thing acted on (the secret). نگشاد is a classical negative past form meaning 'did not open/reveal' (ن‑ + گشاد). بر او marks the indirect object 'to him' ('toward/to him'), and تا چندگاه is a temporal adjunct 'for a while/until some time' modifying the verb.
segments.s174-g67: بدو is the dative 'to him' (to/unto him). گفت functions as 'said' (here reported speech). روزی is a temporal adverb 'one day' or 'once', که introduces the content clause, and اندر جهان is a locative 'in the world' setting the domain/time of what follows.
segments.s174-g68: سخن here means 'a word/thing to say' (i.e. a question or matter). پرسم is first-person (subjunctive/optative) 'may I ask' / 'I ask'. از تو = 'of/from you' marks the source/target of the question. یکی is 'one/a single (thing)'; در نهان is an adverbial phrase 'in private/secretly'. Word order places the object/amount (یکی) before the adverbial (در نهان).
segments.s174-g69: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. ایدون (classical) functions adverbially as 'now/at this time'. که introduces the subordinate clause; یابم is first-person singular subjunctive/potential 'I may find/obtain'. ز تو marks the source 'from you', and راستی is the object 'the truth'.
segments.s174-g70: بشویی is second-person (subjunctive/future-like) 'you will wash/cleanse' used figuratively. به دانش is an instrumental phrase 'by/with knowledge' (به = 'by/with'), دل is the patient 'the heart', and از کاستی means 'of/from deficiency' — i.e. 'to remove deficiencies from the heart'.
segments.s174-g71: ببخشم is a present/future first-person form 'I will give/bestow' (بخشیدن). The clitic -ت (ت) attached marks the indirect object 'to you'; را is the object marker adding emphasis. هرچه خواهی ز من = 'whatever you want from me' (هرچه = 'whatever', خواهی = 'you wish', ز من = 'from me').
segments.s174-g72: ندارم دریغ is a fixed negative phrase 'I have no reluctance / I will not withhold'. از تو marks the target/beneficiary 'toward you / from you (in relation to you)'. پرمایه تن is the thing not withheld: 'one's substantial/ample body/person' (پرمایه = substantial/abundant, تن = body/person). The sentence thus says he will not hesitate to give even his substantial person.
segments.s174-g185: که here begins a free relative clause meaning “who/that.” داند is the classical/present-past 3rd‑person form of دانستن meaning “knows.” به گیتی is a locative poetic phrase “in the world”; the preposition به is used poetically for location. The second که introduces the subordinate content clause “that I am alive.” من زنده‌ام is the 1st‑person present copula construction (زنده + enclitic ام = “I am alive”).
segments.s174-g186: به خاکم gives the first alternative location “in/into the earth” (خاکم literally “my earth,” here ‘the ground/earth’ where one lies). وگر is the conditional/alternative connector “or if / or else.” به‌آتش means “into the fire” and افگنده‌ام is a perfect/result construction meaning “I have been cast/thrown” (passive sense here). Together the clause offers two possible states of the speaker: in the earth or cast into the fire.
segments.s174-g187: سپاس از جهاندار is a fixed phrase “thanks to the Lord of the world” (سپاس = thanks). از here marks the addressee/source of thanks (poetic use ≈ “to/for”). جهاندار is an honorific title ‘lord/sovereign of the world.’ کاین is the fused demonstrative + relative particle (ک + این) meaning “that this,” introducing the following noun phrase رنج سخت (“this harsh suffering”).
segments.s174-g188: به شادی و خوبی is the prepositional goal “into/to joy and goodness.” سرآورد is a verbal form meaning “brought about/produced” (here treated as ‘turned into’). In poetic word order the verb appears before its explicit subject بخت, so بخت is the agent “Fate” (brought about / caused this change).
segments.s174-g189: برفتند is the past 3rd‑person plural “they departed/went.” زآن بیشه = “from that grove” (زآن = from that; بیشه = grove). هر دو = “both (of them).” به راه is the direction phrase “onto the road / on the road.” The sequence places the verb first (poetic narrative order) followed by the source, the reference to the two persons, and the path.
segments.s174-g190: بپرسید here is a classical/perfect form meaning “asked/inquired” (poetic past). خسرو is the subject/name (Khosrow). ز کاووس = “of/from Kavus,” showing whom he questioned; شاه is an appositive/title “the king.” Word order in epic Persian often places the verb before the subject or apposition, as here.
segments.s174-g191: وزآن is a fused conjunction + demonstrative meaning “and/from that” (linking back to the prior event). هفت‌ساله is a compound adjective “seven‑year (long).” غم و درد او = “his sorrow and pain”; او is the 3rd‑person pronoun indicating possession here (“his”), placed after the nouns as is normal in Persian.
segments.s174-g192: This line gives the source of his seven‑year suffering. ز is the preposition “from.” گستردن here is a verbal noun (classical usage) meaning roughly “lying down / spreading/laying out” and in context links with خواب to talk about sleep or the act of going to bed; the exact nuance is idiomatic/classical. و is the conjunction “and.” خواب = “sleep.” وز in this position is an archaic/conjunctive particle (not the wind/weight meanings listed) used to connect clauses; it is not the modern از/و and may be treated as an older “and/from” (sense not in the provided list). خورد is used in the intransitive/impersonal sense “befell/happened to” (i.e., what happened to him in relation to eating or appetite), and او is “him.” Overall: “from (his) lying down and sleep and what befell him in eating” — i.e. loss of sleep and appetite or harms connected with sleep/eating produced his long suffering.
segments.s174-g393: از آن پس is a time phrase 'from that time on' (از = 'from', آن = 'that/then', پس = 'after/then'). گرفتندش is a past transitive verb 'they seized him' with the object clitic -ش attached; the subject 'they' is omitted. اندر میان is a locative phrase 'in/within the midst' (اندر before میان), so the sequence gives 'from then on they seized him in the midst.'
segments.s174-g394: چنان لشکری is a nominal phrase 'such an army' (چنان = 'such', لشکری = 'army/host'). همچو is a simile marker 'like' placed before the comparison; شیر ژیان is a poetic simile 'lions (ژیان used adjectivally/poetically as 'living/ferocious')' describing the army. Word order (noun phrase + همچو + comparison) is typical of poetic similes.
segments.s174-g395: ز (poetic 'from') introduces the source or material: 'from spears'. نیستان is the noun 'reed-bed' and شد (past of شدن) 'became', while آوردگاه is the predicate/complement 'battlefield' — so literally 'from spears the reed-bed became a battlefield.' The verb شد marks change of state.
segments.s174-g396: بپوشید is a past tense verb 'covered/veiled' (ب- prefix here part of the verb form). دیدار خورشید و ماه is the object phrase 'the sight/meeting of sun and moon' (دیدار = 'meeting/sight'); و links خورشید and ماه. The clause means the battlefield/scene 'covered the sight of sun and moon' (i.e., obscured their sight).
segments.s174-g397: غمی شد is a literary/periphrastic construction 'a grief arose' or 'became grief' (غمی noun + شد 'became/occurred'). دل شیر is a genitive/possessive juxtaposition 'the lion's heart' (heart of the lion). در نیستان is the locative 'in the reed‑bed'. Overall: 'a grief came to the lion's heart in the reed‑bed.'
segments.s174-g398: ز خون 'from/with blood' gives the means or material. نیستان is the object 'the reed‑bed' and کرد is the past transitive 'made/caused'. چون is the comparative 'like/as', and میستان here means 'wine‑house/tavern' — so the clause says 'with blood he made the reed‑bed like a wine‑house' (a poetic way to say the reed‑bed was drenched so as to resemble a place of wine and revelry).
segments.s174-g399: از ایشان introduces the affected group 'from/of them'. بیفگند is a past verb 'he threw/cast (down)' — used here as a violent action against opponents. بسیار qualifies the quantity 'many'. گیو (proper name) appears after the verb (poetic word order), so the clause reads 'Giv threw many of them down.'
segments.s174-g400: ستوه آمدند is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'they became weary' (ستوه = 'weary/sick of', آمدند = 'they came' used to form 'became'). آن سواران is the postposed subject 'those horsemen' (subject appears after the verb, common in poetry). ز نیو gives the cause 'from the ney (reed‑flute)' — i.e., 'the horsemen grew weary because of the ney.'
segments.s174-g441: همی هر زمان is an emphatic adverbial phrase meaning 'ever/at every time' (همی + هر زمان). تیز و جوشان are two coordinated adjectives placed before the predicate ('sharp/keen and boiling/ardent'). بدی here is an archaic/persuasive past form meaning 'you were' (2nd person singular past of بودن) — the copula/predicate that links the adjectives to the subject (you).
segments.s174-g442: به نوی is an adverbial phrase meaning 'anew' or 'in a new manner' (به used adverbially + نوی 'new'). چو is the simile particle 'like/as'. پیلی خروشان is a noun + participial/ adjectival phrase 'a (trumpeting/roaring) elephant' — پیلی here 'elephantine' and خروشان 'roaring'. The final بدی again is the past copula 'you were' (archaic spelling); the segment compares the addressee to a roaring elephant.
segments.s174-g443: برآشفت is a past verb 'became angry/was moved to anger' and stands before its logical subject (پیران) — a frequent verb–subject inversion in epic diction. بدو 'to him' is the indirect object; گفت is here the past verb 'said' (though the supplied sense list is for 'conversation' rather than the verb), and بس is an interjection/particle 'enough' functioning as the reported utterance.
segments.s174-g444: که introduces the content/reason clause (here 'for/because') explaining their exclamation. ننگ است is 'it is a shame' (ننگ 'shame' + copula است). از این یاد کردن is a verbal phrase 'to mention/speak of this' where از marks the object/topic ('about/of this') and یاد کردن is the light-verb construction 'to mention' (کردن as auxiliary). به کس 'to anyone' (به = to/for + کس = anyone).
segments.s174-g445: نه at the start negates the clause ('not'). The phrase has inverted order: از یک سوار است چندین سخن literally 'from one rider is many a word' — i.e. 'many words do not come from a single rider.' است is the copula linking the prepositional/source phrase to the nominal predicate. This word order (prepositional phrase + copula + predicate) is typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s174-g446: تو is the (emphatic) subject 'you'. آهنگ آورد is a verbal phrase: آهنگ (here 'melody/song' or 'setting') + آورد (past of آوردن 'bring/cause') — together meaning 'to cause a tune/song/setting (to arise)'. مردان is the direct object 'the men' placed after the verb (poetic word order). مکن is the prohibitive 'do not' (negative imperative) governing the whole clause: 'do not cause the men to take up a song/setting.'
segments.s174-g447: تو رفتی is straightforward past 'you went/you departed' (first clause). و links to the next clause. نستیهن نامور is a nominal phrase where the copula 'شد(ه)' ('became') is omitted — a common elliptic device in poetry — so it reads 'and Nestiḥan (became) renowned.' The order places the proper name before its epithet.
segments.s174-g448: سپاهی is the noun 'an army/warriors' (subject). به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner/behavior' (به used adverbially + کردار 'deed/behavior'). شیران نر is the simile/qualification 'of male lions' (شیران 'lions' followed by the post‑posed adjective نر 'male'); the whole phrase modifies سپاهی to mean 'an army in the bearing/conduct of male lions.'
segments.s174-g289: بدو گفت is a two-part expression: بدو (به + او) is an indirect-object clitic meaning 'to him/unto him', and گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (3rd singular). گیو‌ای شه سرفراز is a vocative address: گیو (proper name) plus ای (vocative particle) and شه سرفراز 'O victorious king' (شه = king, سرفراز = 'victorious/proud'), placed after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s174-g290: سزد is an impersonal predicate 'it befits / it is proper' and introduces a subordinate clause with که. که‌آشکارا (که + آشکارا) is 'that clearly / that openly' (آشکارا as an adverb modifying the clause). بود is the copula 'be' (here expressing the propositional content of the subordinate clause), and بر تو uses بر in a dative/benefactive sense 'to/for you' (تو). راز is the noun 'secret' as the theme of the clause. The whole clause means 'it is fitting that the secret be revealed to you plainly.'
segments.s174-g291: تو is the sentential subject ('you'). از introduces source or provenance: 'from/by virtue of'; ایزدی فر is an adjectival noun phrase 'divine farr' (فر = the royal/divine glory), و links a second coordinated noun برز کیان 'the exaltation/highness of the Kians' (کیان = the Kayan kings). The phrase as a whole gives the source/ground of the subsequent action ('by virtue of divine glory and kingly highness').
segments.s174-g292: به موی is an adverbial phrase 'by a hair' or 'by a hair's breadth' (به + موی), modifying the following verbs. اندر آیی is a verb phrase with آیی (2nd person singular of آمدن in present/optative sense) plus the locative اندر 'into/within' — 'you will come in/enter'. ببینی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive of دیدن used to indicate what will be (often future in sense): here it goes with میان ('in the midst/among') to yield 'you will (be) seen/appear in their midst' or 'you shall be among them and be seen there.' Poetic word order places the adverbial before the verbs; the pair اندر آیی / ببینی gives a resultive sequence 'you will come in ... and be seen in the middle.'
segments.s174-g293: بدو گفت as before: بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said'. زین اسپ فرخ نژاد is a compact noun phrase: زین here functions in an archaic/poetic sense pointing to 'this' or 'of this' (often written زین/ز آن in older Persian), followed by اسپ 'steed/horse' and the adjective compound فرخ نژاد 'of fortunate/fair lineage' modifying اسپ. Together it names 'this fair‑bred steed' (or loosely 'from this steed').
segments.s174-g294: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / one', serving as the subject of the clause. بر دل is a prepositional expression 'upon the heart' (بر = upon; دل = heart). اندیشه 'thought/remembrance' is the noun that 'came' (یاد is synonymous and follows as appositive/emphatic). آمدت is the past tense 'came' with the 2nd-person enclitic ت indicating 'to you' (i.e., 'it occurred to you'). یاد is the noun 'remembrance/memory' completing the idea: 'a certain remembrance came upon your heart.'
segments.s174-g295: چنین بود is a fronted adverbial phrase 'thus was' or 'it was thus' with بود the past copula. اندیشه پهلوان is a genitive/appositive structure 'the thought of the champion' (پهلوان postmodifies اندیشه): together 'thus was the champion's thought.' This word order (adverbial + copula + noun phrase) is typical of classical poetic style.
segments.s174-g296: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. اهریمن is 'a demon / the devil' (here a hostile supernatural being). آمد is simple past 'came', and بر takes the sense 'upon/against' to mark the target of the action; این جوان 'this young man' is the prepositional object (the one afflicted). So the clause reads 'that a demon came upon this youth.'
segments.s174-g33: 'جهان آفرید و مکان و زمان' is a simple clause: subject 'جهان' (world) + past verb 'آفرید' (created), followed by a coordination 'و مکان و زمان' ('and place and time'). Word order is Subject–Verb–(Objects/Complements) typical of Persian poetry; the two nouns after the conjunction are direct objects or items listed as created.
segments.s174-g34: 'پی پشه خرد و پیل گران' continues the list with 'پی' meaning 'after/then' (classical/poetic usage). 'پشه خرد' is a noun + adjective ('pashé' + 'khord' = 'tiny/insignificant gnat'), then 'و پیل گران' ('and the heavy elephant') coordinates another noun + adjective. Adjectives follow the nouns here (typical in classical Persian), and 'پی' links this pair to what precedes ('and then').
segments.s174-g35: 'چو' here means 'when' (introducing a temporal clause). 'سالار ترکان' is the compound noun 'commander (سالار) of the Turks (ترکان)'. 'به دل گفت' is an idiom 'said to his heart' = 'thought to himself'; 'گفت' is past tense 'said'. The final 'من' begins the quoted thought ('I...').
segments.s174-g36: 'به بیشی برآرم سر از انجمن' is the inner thought introduced by 'من' — a first-person verbal phrase. 'به بیشی' = 'with excess/pride' (بیشی here = excess/more), 'برآرم' is the 1st-person singular present/subjunctive form of برآوردن used in classical Persian to express 'I will raise / I shall lift', 'سر' is the direct object 'head', and 'از انجمن' 'above/from the assembly' shows the target/limit: 'raise my head above the assembly'. The verb appears before its object for emphasis (poetic inversion), and the whole is a reported inner speech.
segments.s174-g37: 'چنان' = 'thus/so' is an adverb commenting on the manner; 'شاهزاده جوان را' is a marked definite object ('را' marks the direct object 'the young prince'); 'بکشت' is an archaic epic past-form (prefix بـ + past stem کشت) meaning 'he killed' or 'he slew'. The object precedes the verb, as is common in Persian with the object marker 'را'.
segments.s174-g38: 'ندانست جز گنج و شمشیر پشت' is a negative past clause: 'ندانست' = 'he did not know' (or 'he was ignorant of/considered nothing'), followed by 'جز' = 'except'. The exception list 'گنج و شمشیر پشت' = 'treasure and the sword (at his) back' — 'پشت' here denotes location 'behind/at the back', so the line means he knew/valued nothing except riches and his sword on his back.
segments.s174-g39: 'هم از پشت او روشن کردگار' uses a poetic, somewhat inverted word order. 'هم' = 'also/likewise'; 'از پشت او' = 'from his back'; 'روشن' is an adjective 'luminous/clear' placed before 'کردگار' (the Creator) for emphasis; here 'کردگار' is the subject (the Creator/God). The inversion (prepositional phrase + adjective + subject) is stylistic, common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s174-g40: 'درختی برآورد یازان به بار' completes the clause: 'درختی' (a tree, indefinite object) + past verb 'برآورد' ('brought forth/produced'). 'یازان' is a participial/adjectival word meaning 'spreading/widening' modifying the tree, and 'به بار' is a poetic expression 'bearing fruit' (the whole phrase means the Creator produced a tree that spread and bore fruit). The verb 'برآورد' is used in the classical past sense 'brought forth'.
segments.s174-g273: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. بنشست is past tense (ب- prefix + نشست) 'he sat down'. بر باره is a prepositional phrase 'on the pack/saddle' (بر + باره). بفشارد is the past action 'he pressed/squeezed' (classical preverb ب- + فشرد) and ران is the body-part object 'thigh' — together meaning he urged/pressed with his thigh against the mount.
segments.s174-g274: برآمد ز جا is an idiomatic verb phrase 'rose up / got up from its place' (برآمد + prepositional phrase ز جا). آن هیون گران is a demonstrative noun phrase 'that heavy beast' (آن + هیون + adjective گران); note the adjective follows the noun as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s174-g275: به کردار باد is a simile 'in the manner of the wind' (به + کردار + باد). هوا is the acting subject 'the air / wind' and بردمید is a past verb 'carried off / snatched away' (بردمید, classical perfective). The whole segment means the air/wind carried him off like a gust.
segments.s174-g276: بپرید is a past action 'he sprang up / flew off' (classical ب- + پرید). وز here means 'by/from that gust/blast' (poetic ز/وز used adverbially), followed by the name گیو (Giv) as subject and شد ناپدید 'became vanished' (شد + adjective ناپدید) meaning 'he disappeared.'
segments.s174-g277: غمی شد دل گیو is a predicative construction: غمی (a grief) + شد 'became' with the logical subject دل گیو 'Giv's heart' → 'a grief came upon Giv's heart'. و خیره بماند: خیره 'stunned/astonished' + بماند (classical past-form of ماندن) 'he remained' → 'and he stood stunned.'
segments.s174-g278: بدان is the fused form meaning 'in/with that' (به + آن → بدان) introducing the circumstance 'in that astonishment' (خیرگی). نام یزدان بخواند is a verb phrase: نام یزدان 'the name of God' and بخواند (past) 'he called/invoked' — so 'in that stupor he called on the name of God.' Note: the tokenization provided splits بدان into ب + دان; here they function together as the adverbial 'بدان'.
segments.s174-g279: همی گفت is iterative past 'he kept saying / he would say'. که introduces reported content 'that'. آهرمن (Ahriman) is the subject and چاره‌جوی is a deverbal/adjectival compound 'seeker of a remedy' modifying Ahriman — 'Ahriman, seeking a remedy.'
segments.s174-g280: یکی بارگی گشت: یکی 'a certain / one' + بارگی 'leaf/folliage' + گشت (past of گشتن) 'became / turned into' — 'he turned into a single leaf.' و بنمود روی: و 'and' + بنمود (past) 'displayed / showed' + روی 'face' — 'and showed his face.' The sequence describes a metamorphosis into a leaf and then revealing his face.
segments.s174-g265: همی داشت is a progressive/perfective narrative construction: همی (an intensive/continuous adverb) + داشت (past of داشتن used here as an imperfective auxiliary) yields ‘was keeping/was holding’. در آبخور is a locative prepositional phrase ‘in/at the watering-place’. پای خویش is the possessed noun ‘its foot’ (پای + the reflexive/possessive خویش split here into خوی + ش), with the possessive clitic attached.
segments.s174-g266: از آنجا که here introduces a causal clause 'since/because there', with از taking the causal sense and که reinforcing the subordination. بد describes the condition there (‘bad/unfavorable’). دست ننهاد پیش is a simple past negated verb phrase: ننهاد is the past negative of نهادن ‘to place/extend’ and پیش ‘forward/in front’ indicates direction — ‘did not put/extend (its) foreleg forward.’
segments.s174-g267: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. کیخسرو is the subject/name. او را is the definite object (او marked by را). به آرام is an adverbial phrase 'in calmness/at rest' (به + noun). یافت is the past-tense verb 'found' (یافتن). The clause is a standard temporal subordinate clause: 'When Ki-Khosrow found him at rest...'.
segments.s174-g268: بپویید is a past narrative form of پوییدن/پوییدن with the prefix بـ- showing simple action: 'he went/approached' or 'he sought'. و 'and' links the verbs. با زین سوی او is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with (the) saddle, toward him' (با = with; زین = saddle; سوی او = toward him). شتافت is the past tense 'he hastened/rushed', the final main verb of the coordinated action.
segments.s174-g269: بمالید is the past tense of مالیدن 'to rub'. The prepositional phrase بر چشم او (on its eye) shows the target of the rubbing. دست و روی ('hand and face') are coordinated nouns that function as the instrument/what is moved in the action — i.e. he rubbed with his hand upon its eye/face (pragmatically: he rubbed the animal's eye with his hand and stroked its face).
segments.s174-g270: The clause continues with another action: بر + یال (on the mane) with ببسود as the verb meaning here a poetic/archaic action 'to stroke/smooth/treat' (used transitively over the mane). و coordinates a further small clause: بشخود ('to/for himself', reflexive) + موی ('hair') — i.e. he took/handled a lock of hair for himself (physically plucked or twisted it). Note the verbs and particles are compressed poetic forms.
segments.s174-g271: لگامش (لگام + possessive clitic) is 'his bridle'. بدو 'to him' (دِ+او compressed form) marks the indirect object. داد is the past tense 'gave'. و connects the coordinated action. زین بر نهاد is 'placed/put the saddle on (him)': زین 'saddle', بر 'on', نهاد past of نهادن 'to place/put'. The subject is implied and carries over from the narrative.
segments.s174-g272: بسی is an intensifier 'much/very' (an adverbial quantifier). از پدر یاد کردن is an idiom expressed here as از پدر (of/from his father) + یاد (remembrance) + کرد (did/made) — i.e. 'he caused/remained in much remembrance of his father'. با درد is an adverbial phrase 'with pain/with sorrow' modifying the remembering. Overall: 'he remembered his father greatly, in sorrow.'
segments.s174-g337: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' (archaic/poetic use). افتاد is the simple past of افتادن used impersonally ('it befell' / 'fell to hand'); together چو افتاد = 'when it happened / when it fell'. بر is the preposition 'upon/on' linking the verb to the following object phrase. خواسته is an adjectival/nominal form 'desired' ('that which is desired') and sits before چشم to form 'the eye's desire'. چشم here means 'gaze/eye' (the organ or look) and with خواسته yields 'what the eye desired'. گیو is the proper name Giv, the possessor of that eye; the whole phrase therefore means 'when that which Giv's eye desired fell (to him)'.
segments.s174-g338: گزین کرد is a two‑word past construction: گزین (choose) + کرد (did/made) = 'he chose/selected' (past). The noun phrase درع سیاووش نیو follows as the direct object (no را here because epic Persian often omits it); درع is 'armor', سیاووش is a post‑nominal proper name used attributively ('of Siavash'), and نیو is the postpositive adjective 'new' (regional/poetic form). Word order (noun + possessor + adjective) is typical in Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s174-g339: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' indicating source. گوهر is 'jewel/gem' and is the noun governed by ز. که introduces a relative clause 'which/that', here linking to the following predicate. پرمایه‌تر is the comparative adjective 'more substantial / richer' placed before the verb (typical order in older Persian). یافتند is the past plural 'they found / they obtained' and completes the relative clause: 'the gems which they found to be richer'.
segments.s174-g340: ببردند is the past plural of بردن 'they took / carried off' and is the main verb of this clause. چندان is an adverb 'to that extent / so much' specifying amount. که here functions as a subordinating conjunction 'that/so that' introducing the limit clause. برتافتند is past plural 'they could bear / carry' (from برتافتن), so چنان که برتافتند = 'as much as they could bear/carry'; together the clause means they took as much as they were able to carry.
