segments.s174-g341: همان is the demonstrative 'that same' / 'the very'. ترگ is a small cloth-piece 'rag / shred' and here names one item; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. پرمایه is an adjective/noun meaning 'substantial, valuable' describing the other item. برگستوان is a verbal/lexical form 'returned / were returned' (poetic/older form of 'برگشتن' / 'بازگشتن') and functions as the main verb linking these items to the action of giving/returning in this line; syntactically the phrase presents the items which were returned.
segments.s174-g342: سلیحی is the noun 'a weapon/armament' (the -ی ending here is an attributive/indefinite form) and is immediately followed by a relative clause. که is the relative pronoun 'which/that' introducing the clause. بود is the past copula 'was' (here 'that was located / that belonged'). از is the preposition 'from/of', and در in this context means 'door/gate' (not merely 'in'); پهلوان is 'the champion/hero'. The relative clause therefore specifies 'the weapon that had been at the hero's gate / belonged to the hero's household'.
segments.s174-g343: سر گنج را is a fronted object phrase marked by را for definiteness/emphasis: سر 'chief/head' of گنج 'treasure' = 'the chief of the treasure' or 'the treasure's head'. شاه is the subject 'the king'. کرد is the past verb 'made/did' and takes the following adjective as complement. استوار is the predicate adjective 'steadfast/firm' — the structure 'X را Y کرد Z' here means 'Y made X Z' (object fronted for emphasis, subject follows object as is common in epic Persian).
segments.s174-g344: به راه بیابان is a prepositional phrase 'for/to the desert road' (به + راه + بیابان = 'toward/for the desert way'), specifying purpose or destination. برآراست is a past verb meaning 'he arranged / adorned / set in order' (poetic compound verb); it governs the following noun. کار is the object 'work / affairs / preparations' so the clause means 'he arranged the work for the desert road' — a practical ordering of affairs for travel across the desert.
segments.s174-g105: یکی ... و یکی is a classical way to introduce two distinct individuals: 'one (is) X and one (is) Y.' Here each یکی is an indefinite/identifying 'one' introducing a proper name (two people). The conjunction و simply links the two name phrases.
segments.s174-g106: This clause has the pattern [مرا بخت بر گنبد افشاند ...]: مرا (me) is the object, بخت (fortune/fate) is the actor, and افشاند is a past/perfect third‑person verb 'threw/scattered.' بر گنبد is a prepositional phrase 'upon the dome/crown (i.e. head).' The final lexical item (گوز) is obscure or corrupt here; in the line it functions as an appended word after the verb and should be treated as anomalous or emphatic.
segments.s174-g107: همی + verb (برفشانم) is a poetic habitual/progressive construction: همی gives the sense 'I continually/keeps on ...' and برفشانم is first‑person present/habitual 'I scatter' (classical subjunctive form used as present). به introduces the beneficiary/target 'to/for the ...' and خیره روان describes those affected: خیره 'staring/astonished' modifying روان (here describing the moving/attending people), i.e. 'to the astonished onlookers.'
segments.s174-g108: خمیده‌ست پشتم is a nominal clause with the predicate خمیده‌ست (خمیده + است) 'is bent/curved' and the subject/topic پشتم 'my back' (پشت + ـم enclitic 'my'). چو introduces a simile 'like/as' and خم کمان 'the curve of the bow' completes it: 'my back is bent like the bow's curve.'
segments.s174-g109: همانا که is an emphatic formula 'verily/indeed that...' introducing the clause. خسرو is the subject; ز مادر uses the poetic preposition ز 'from/of' and نزاد is an archaic past/intransitive form meaning 'was born' (classical usage). The clause simply states 'Khosrow was born of a mother.'
segments.s174-g110: وگر at the start of a clause is a conditional/concessive 'and if/but if.' زاد is another past form 'was born' (here repeating or remarking on birth). دادش is composed of داد 'gave' plus the enclitic pronoun ش 'him' = 'Time gave him'; زمانه (Time/the world/fortune) is the agent, and به باد دادن is an idiom 'to give to the wind,' i.e. to ruin or cast away. The sense is concessive: even if he was born, Time has cast him away.
segments.s174-g111: ز جستن مرا is an infinitival/gerundial phrase: ز + جستن (to seek) with مرا 'me' as the object — 'to seek me' / 'from seeking me.' رنج و سختی‌ست is a nominal predicate 'there is suffering and hardship' (سختی‌ست = 'it is hardship' with the copula). بهر at the end of the segment is a preposition 'for/on behalf of/with regard to' linking to the following segment.
segments.s174-g112: انوشه is a proper name (Anoushe). کسی is an indefinite 'someone' or 'a man.' کاو here is an archaic/formal element that functions like a relative ('that/who') in classical Persian (often equivalent to که او); بمیرد is a verb formed with the old prefix بـ for the present/subjunctive sense 'would die' / 'dies,' and به زهر is the instrument/prepositional phrase 'by (with) poison.' Together: 'Anoushe — someone who will die by poison.'
segments.s174-g145: ز is the preposition 'from/of' placed before the names; کشواد و گیو‌ت is simply two proper names joined by و 'and'. که here functions as an interrogative/relative 'who/that' introducing the question clause. داد is the past-tense verb 'gave' and آگهی the noun 'news/information' — together داد آگهی = 'brought/gave news'. The word order (preposition + names, then که + verb + object) is typical of poetic inversion.
segments.s174-g146: که begins a relative clause describing that messenger ('who'). با is the preposition 'with' and خرمی is an adverbial/nominal phrase 'merriment, joy' ('with joy'). بادی is a poetic descriptor ('wind(-borne) / swift, like the wind') and و links it to فرهی ('noble / glorious'); the verb is omitted (ellipsis) and must be supplied from context (e.g. 'came' or 'was'), a common poetic economy.
segments.s174-g147: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto him' (marking the addressee). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said'; the subject follows the verb (postverbal subject), here کیخسرو ('KiKhosrow'). ای شیر مرد is a vocative phrase ('O brave/valiant man') addressing the listener. Verb–subject–vocative order like this is common in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s174-g148: مرا (here split orthographically as م + را) is the oblique/accusative 'me' ('to me'); را is the definite/object marker attached in the phrase. مادر is the subject 'mother', این 'this/the foregoing' is the thing mentioned, از پدر 'about/from (my) father' gives the topic, and یاد کرد is the compound meaning 'mentioned/remembered' (یاد + کردن, past tense کرد). Word order places the indirect object (مرا) before the subject; یاد کرد functions as the main verbal expression 'recounted/said'.
segments.s174-g149: که is a relative/continuative 'who/that'. از فر یزدان literally 'of/from the divine farr' — فر یزدان = 'divine/royal glory' (a technical, poetic concept). گشادی سخن is a fixed poetic phrasing meaning 'lengthy/extended speech' (گشادی 'broad/extended' modifying سخن 'speech'): together 'spoke at length about the divine glory.'
segments.s174-g150: بدان‌گه = 'on that occasion/at that time'. که introduces a subordinate temporal clause 'when/that'. اندرز‑ش is 'اندرز' (advice/counsel) with the enclitic ‑ش 'his' = 'his counsel'. آمد is the past 'came' and به بن 'to the root/base' is idiomatic: 'آمد به بن' = 'took root / became established.' The clause thus means 'at that time his counsel took root.'
segments.s174-g151: همی is the iterative/imperfective particle signalling habitual or continuous action ('used to', 'kept on'). گفت is 'said' (past) but with همی it renders a habitual past meaning. با + نامور مادر + م (here orthographically split) = 'to my famed/renowned mother' — با marks the person addressed and the final ‑م on مادر is the possessive 'my'.
segments.s174-g152: کز is a relative/interrogative 'that/from which' used to introduce the content of the question. ایدر 'here', چه 'what' (interrogative), آید is the present/future sense 'will come' (classical Persian uses آمد/آید forms for eventuality), ز بد 'from/because of evil' (source), and بر سرم 'upon my head' = 'upon me' (سرم = 'my head', with possessive suffix). Together: 'What evil will come upon me from here?'

sentences.s174-s229: Let the hissing/whistle be witness enough for you; no man fought — neither Gupāl nor the drum.
sentences.s174-s189: Giv vaulted onto his horse's back, and a tune like the men's reed‑pipe sounded.
sentences.s174-s190: He had armor across his chest and a rag upon his head; his heart was blood‑red and his body laid down by death.
sentences.s174-s191: When he saw the host's dust from afar, he struck his hand and drew his sword from between (his side/sheath).
sentences.s174-s192: He raised a roar like a cloud, so that Hejbar's mind and sight grew dark.
sentences.s174-s45: He kept roaming around the desert and the mountains, enduring hardship and difficulty, far from his company.
sentences.s174-s46: He was so miserable that one day, full of thought, there was before him a fruitful grove.
sentences.s174-s47: Into that meadow came a sullen one; the world was joyful, and the man's heart (was) given to sorrow.
sentences.s174-s48: He saw green earth and a spring full of water; he saw also a place of rest and sleep.
